html:
Animal Farm — 10 Most Likely Questions

AQA English Literature Paper 2 — Section A (Modern Texts). 30 marks + 4 marks AO4 (SPaG). Recommended writing time: 50 minutes. AOs: AO1 (response + textual reference) / AO2 (writer's methods) / AO3 (context) / AO4 (SPaG). NO extract — questions are essay-stems only.

How to use this document

Each question prints a typical AQA stem, a one-paragraph frame, a short technique gloss, and a skeleton plan in Sophicly's locked structure (Introduction 3 sentences / three Body Paragraphs of 7 sentences / Conclusion 4 sentences). Memorise the structure once and the same shape carries every essay. Memorise the cross-question reuse table at the end and the same chapter-bank answers all ten Qs.

The seven-sentence body paragraph shape

1. Topic sentence — concept only, no techniques named.

2. Technique + evidence — one quote with multiple rhetorical techniques visible inside it.

3. Close analysis — grammar, word-class, syntax, and one further observation about the same quote.

4. Effect on reader 1 — what the reader sees, hears, feels.

5. Effect on reader 2 — what the reader recognises, registers, infers.

6. Author's purpose — what Orwell may be arguing through this scene.

7. Context (AO3) — one period-specific fact that lands the BP back at the controlling concept.

Memorise these once

Animal Farm argues that every revolution that ignores the corruptibility of those who lead it ends as the tyranny it overthrew — power without accountability is the disease that recurs across regimes, and language is the first thing the new tyranny corrupts to disguise what it is doing. Orwell sets the novella as a beast fable so that the operations of Stalinist totalitarianism — the rewriting of history, the cult of personality, the public executions, the betrayal of the working class — can be recognised in animal form by any reader, and so that the diagnosis lands as universal warning rather than topical polemic. The original revolutionary diagnosis was honourable; the betrayal was historical, not inherent — and the reader who closes the novella unable to tell pig from man has been taught to demand of every leadership the accountability mechanism Old Major's vision did not specify.

Key terms:

• Beast fable — short prose form using animal protagonists to deliver moral or political instruction (Aesop, La Fontaine, Swift); Orwell's chosen mode for Animal Farm.

• Allegorical tragedy — the structure in which a representative figure (Boxer for the working class, Snowball for the visionary intelligentsia) is consumed by the regime they helped build.

• Cyclical structure — the novella opens at Manor Farm, becomes Animal Farm, and is renamed Manor Farm by Chapter 10; the form is the message.

• Free indirect discourse — narrative voice fused with a character's consciousness; Orwell's preferred channel for Clover's silent recognition in Chapter 7.

• Newspeak / political language — Orwell's argument in "Politics and the English Language" (1946) that vague, euphemistic prose is the linguistic instrument of totalitarianism; Animal Farm's lucid prose enacts the alternative.

• Allegorical map — Manor Farm = pre-revolutionary Russia; Jones = Tsar Nicholas II; Old Major = Marx + Lenin; Napoleon = Stalin; Snowball = Trotsky; Squealer = Pravda / Soviet propaganda; Boxer = the loyal working class; Dogs = NKVD; Sheep = propagandised masses; Pilkington = Britain; Frederick = Nazi Germany; Battle of the Cowshed = Russian Civil War; Battle of the Windmill = Operation Barbarossa; Windmill = Five-Year Plans; rewritten commandments = Stalinist rewriting of history.

---

Q1 — Napoleon

Question

How does Orwell use the character of Napoleon to explore ideas about totalitarianism in Animal Farm?

Write about:

- how Orwell presents Napoleon - how Orwell uses Napoleon to explore ideas about totalitarianism.

[30 marks + 4 AO4 = 34 marks]

Frame

Napoleon is totalitarianism made flesh — the silent boar whose accumulation of personal force begins at the moment of Old Major's death, escalates through the secret training of the dogs, and completes itself at the closing dinner where the pig and the man become indistinguishable. The plan tracks three stages of the same arc — Chapters 1–4 silent positioning beneath democratic theatre, Chapters 5–7 violent seizure and the rewriting of history, Chapters 8–10 the personality cult and the closing dinner.

Technique gloss:

• Litotes — affirmation through deliberate understatement ("not much of a talker").

• Allegorical naming — the post-revolutionary autocrat Napoleon Bonaparte (1799 / 1804) named into the boar's identity.

• Dramatic irony — the reader recognises Napoleon's strategic silence as the thing the animals miss.

• Euphemism — "education" concealing the militarisation of the puppies.

• Pluperfect rewriting — Squealer's "had seemed to oppose" converting opposition into foresight.

• Symbolic action — Napoleon's whip in his trotter (Chapter 10) condensing the controlling concept into a single image.

INTRODUCTION (3 sentences — about 115 words)

• Hook (historical fact): Joseph Stalin was elected General Secretary of the Soviet Communist Party in April 1922 — a clerical post Lenin and Trotsky considered beneath the visionary leadership of the revolution — and used the bureaucratic machinery of that office to consolidate personal control before either rival recognised what had happened.

• Building sentence (historical context): Orwell published Animal Farm in August 1945 after rejection by Faber (T. S. Eliot), Gollancz and Cape on the grounds that the Soviet Union was Britain's wartime ally — and the boar Napoleon, named for the post-revolutionary autocrat who declared himself Emperor in 1804, was Orwell's lucid argument that Stalinism had reproduced the Tsarist hierarchy under new vocabulary.

• Thesis: Across his Chapters 1–4 silent positioning beneath the democratic theatre of the Sunday meetings, his Chapters 5–7 violent seizure of authority through the dogs and the rewriting of Snowball's role at the Battle of the Cowshed, and his Chapters 8–10 personality cult that culminates in the closing dinner where the pig becomes indistinguishable from the man, Orwell presents Napoleon as the architectural anatomy of totalitarianism — power accumulated in silence, enforced through terror, and disguised through language.

BODY PARAGRAPH 1 — Chapters 1–4: silent positioning (7 sentences — about 230 words)

Scene focus: the post-revolution Sunday meetings; Napoleon takes the puppies away from their mothers in Chapter 3 "to make himself responsible for their education".

• Topic sentence: Orwell installs Napoleon's totalitarian project not as confrontation with the revolution but as quiet positioning beneath it — the figure who is already building his enforcement apparatus while the rest of the farm believes the revolution has already been won.

• Technique + evidence: Orwell deploys litotes inside dramatic irony — Napoleon described as "a large, rather fierce-looking Berkshire boar, the only Berkshire on the farm, not much of a talker, but with a reputation for getting his own way" (Chapter 2) — combining litotes (the genteel understatement "not much of a talker" hiding the substance of "getting his own way") with dramatic irony (the reader, on rereading, recognises that the silence the animals dismiss as ordinariness is the strategic refusal of debate that totalitarian leaders cultivate).

• Close analysis: The qualifier "the only Berkshire" isolates Napoleon morphologically from the other pigs — already differentiated from the species — while the noun "reputation" hands the warning to the animals who will then ignore it, the syntax of the sentence mimicking the political negligence Orwell is diagnosing.

• Effect on reader 1: Thus the reader hears the disarming domesticity of "not much of a talker" and feels the scene's surface invitation to dismiss Napoleon — the same dismissal the Soviet Communist Party gave the new General Secretary in 1922.

• Effect on reader 2: Indeed, the reader infers, on second pass, that the silence the animals dismiss is the precondition of the seizure to come — that totalitarian leaders accumulate authority not through the speeches their rivals attend to but through the bureaucratic and physical preparation their rivals do not.

• Author's purpose: Orwell may be arguing through Napoleon's silent positioning that totalitarianism does not arrive in costume — that the animal who would later carry a whip in his trotter was, at the start, the one the others noticed least, and that political vigilance therefore demands attention to the figures who do not yet appear dangerous.

• Context (AO3): In April 1922 Stalin was made General Secretary of the Soviet Communist Party in a clerical role that Trotsky and others considered beneath visionary leadership; from that office Stalin built the personal control that, by 1929, drove Trotsky into exile and, by 1938, executed almost every original Bolshevik — the silent positioning Napoleon enacts in Chapter 2 is the historical record condensed into a single sentence.

BODY PARAGRAPH 2 — Chapters 5–7: violent seizure and rewriting (7 sentences — about 235 words)

Scene focus: the dogs drive Snowball off the farm in Chapter 5; the public executions in Chapter 7; Squealer's rewriting of Snowball at the Battle of the Cowshed.

• Topic sentence: Where Chapters 1–4 had positioned Napoleon beneath the revolutionary theatre, Chapters 5–7 deploy the apparatus he had cultivated in private — the dogs as enforcement, the rewriting of the historical record as ideology, and the public executions as pedagogy — completing the seizure of the revolution's instruments by a single boar who never wins an argument.

• Technique + evidence: Orwell deploys auditory escalation paired with militarised description — "At this there was a terrible baying sound outside, and nine enormous dogs wearing brass-studded collars came bounding into the barn. They dashed straight for Snowball" (Chapter 5) — combining onomatopoeia (the "baying" arriving before the bodies, the sound the only warning the animals receive) with militarised metonymy (the "brass-studded collars" converting the puppies of Chapter 3 into the secret-police uniforms of Chapter 5) inside the same sentence.

• Close analysis: The adjective "enormous" registers the escalation of scale — the puppies once seized for "education" now rendered as terror-instruments — while the verb "dashed" eliminates the temporal space for democratic response, the sentence enacting the speed at which a totalitarian coup overtakes the institution it captures.

• Effect on reader 1: Thus the reader feels the pastoral tone of the previous chapters shatter — the violence visceral and unanswerable — generating the same frozen bewilderment the animals themselves register.

• Effect on reader 2: Indeed, the reader recognises the structural method of the totalitarian coup — that the new regime does not defeat the revolution it captures through argument but consumes the revolution's institutions overnight using force prepared in secret.

• Author's purpose: Orwell may be demonstrating that the totalitarian regime arrives not gradually but suddenly — that the long quiet of Chapters 1–4 was the preparation, and that the dogs of Chapter 5 are what the silence had been building all along.

• Context (AO3): Stalin expelled Trotsky from the Communist Party in November 1927 and exiled him from the Soviet Union in 1929; the NKVD secret police, under Yagoda and then Yezhov, conducted the public show trials of Zinoviev and Kamenev in 1936 and Bukharin in 1938 — the dogs in Chapter 5 and the throat-tearing executions in Chapter 7 condense those operations into the barn floor.

BODY PARAGRAPH 3 — Chapters 8–10: personality cult and closing dinner (7 sentences — about 235 words)

Scene focus: Napoleon's accumulating titles in Chapter 8; the closing dinner with Pilkington in Chapter 10; the pigs walking on two legs.

• Topic sentence: Where Chapters 5–7 had completed the seizure through terror, Chapters 8–10 complete the disguise through language — the personality cult inflating Napoleon beyond accountability, the closing dinner with Pilkington enacting the alliance with the very humans the revolution was meant to overthrow, and the closing image of pig and man indistinguishable announcing that totalitarianism's ultimate achievement is the indistinguishability of itself from the regime it replaced.

• Technique + evidence: Orwell deploys cataloguing satire inside passive construction — Napuleon's titles accumulate as "our Leader, Comrade Napoleon", "Father of All Animals", "Terror of Mankind", "Protector of the Sheepfold", "Ducklings' Friend" (Chapter 8) — combining cataloguing satire (the absurd inflation of grandiose epithets exposing the linguistic mechanism by which a single leader is raised beyond rational evaluation) with passive construction ("was always referred to", the agentless grammar making the cult appear organic rather than manufactured) inside the same naming sequence.

• Close analysis: The escalating ridicule of "Ducklings' Friend" lands precisely because it occupies the same syntactic slot as "Terror of Mankind" — the cataloguing flattening sentimentality and brutality into a single grammatical list, exposing the regime's appetite for total reverence regardless of contradiction.

• Effect on reader 1: Thus the reader laughs at the absurdity of the titles and then registers the unease — the laughter and the dread occupying the same sentence — recognising the pedagogical method of a regime that uses linguistic excess to insulate authority from criticism.

• Effect on reader 2: Indeed, the reader perceives the closing dinner as the historical Tehran Conference of November 1943 condensed — Stalin, Churchill and Roosevelt seated together — and registers Orwell's most disturbing claim: that totalitarianism's victory is not the destruction of its enemy but its acceptance into the same room as an equal.

• Author's purpose: Orwell may be arguing that totalitarianism is most dangerous when its language has become indistinguishable from the language it replaced — that the closing image of pig and man, "impossible to say which was which" (Chapter 10), is not the failure of the revolution but the achievement of the regime that consumed it.

• Context (AO3): Stalin adopted Tsarist trappings — military uniforms, palatial residences, hierarchical titles — across the late 1930s and 1940s; the November 1943 Tehran Conference saw him received as co-architect of the post-war order alongside Churchill and Roosevelt; Animal Farm's closing dinner is that historical recognition rendered as fable.

CONCLUSION (4 sentences — about 145 words)

• Restated thesis: In conclusion, Orwell constructs Napoleon as the architectural anatomy of totalitarianism — silent positioning in Chapters 1–4, violent seizure in Chapters 5–7, personality cult and closing dinner in Chapters 8–10 — exposing through one boar the precise mechanism by which an unaccountable revolution reproduces the regime it overthrew.

• Controlling concept: What Chapter 2 installed as the silent reputation for "getting his own way", Chapter 5 enacted through the dogs, Chapter 10 confirmed through the whip in the trotter — three stages of the same totalitarian project, the consistency of the diagnosis confirming Orwell's claim that power without accountability inevitably reproduces tyranny.

• Author's purpose: Writing for a 1945 readership about to enter the Cold War, Orwell uses Napoleon to argue that the diagnosis must outlast the historical occasion — that every leadership, in every era, must specify the mechanisms by which it will be checked before it acquires the office Stalin built into a personal regime.

• Ultimate moral: Napoleon's enduring force as a fable creation is that he is the reasonable-looking boar who, in Chapter 2, the animals had no reason to fear — and Orwell's warning is therefore directed not at the leaders the reader has already learned to distrust but at the ones the reader has not yet thought to.

Q2 — Snowball

Question

How does Orwell use the character of Snowball to explore ideas about revolution and idealism in Animal Farm?

Write about:

- how Orwell presents Snowball - how Orwell uses Snowball to explore ideas about revolution and idealism.

[30 marks + 4 AO4 = 34 marks]

Frame

Snowball is the visionary intelligentsia consumed by the regime he helped build — articulate, brave, principled, and, by the structural logic of unaccountable revolution, the figure the strongman must remove first. The plan tracks three stages of the same erasure — Chapters 1–4 vision and military leadership, Chapters 5–6 expulsion by the dogs, Chapters 7–10 retroactive rewriting into traitor.

Technique gloss:

• Allegorical naming — Snowball: fragile, easily destroyed, melts on contact with heat or hostile ground; the visionary's vulnerability to the strongman.

• Foil pairing — Snowball against Napoleon: debate against decision, persuasion against force.

• Free indirect discourse — narrative voice fused with the animals' confused account of the Battle of the Cowshed.

• Pluperfect tense — "had seemed", "had been" — the grammatical instrument of historical falsification.

• Anaphora — Squealer's repeated "comrades" hiding the rewriting of Snowball's record.

• Antithesis — Snowball's debating eloquence set against Napoleon's terse refusal.

INTRODUCTION (3 sentences — about 115 words)

• Hook (historical fact): Leon Trotsky led the Red Army during the Russian Civil War of 1918–22 and was Lenin's named co-leader of the October Revolution; by 1940 he had been driven from the Communist Party in 1927, exiled from the Soviet Union in 1929, and assassinated in Mexico City in August 1940 by an NKVD agent wielding an ice-axe.

• Building sentence (historical context): Orwell, who had served in the POUM militia in Spain in 1936–37 and watched Stalinist forces suppress his own faction in Barcelona's May Days, was one of very few British writers in 1944 willing to insist that Trotsky's elimination was not the revolution's defence but its fundamental betrayal — and Snowball is Orwell's clearest dramatisation of that biographical conviction.

• Thesis: Across his Chapters 1–4 visionary leadership in articulating Animalism and his bravery at the Battle of the Cowshed, his Chapter 5 expulsion by the dogs the moment his proposals threaten Napoleon's authority, and his Chapters 7–10 retroactive rewriting into traitor whose "secret agency for Jones" the sheep are taught to bleat, Orwell presents Snowball as the structural casualty of unaccountable revolution — the visionary the regime erases because the regime's survival requires the visionary's silence.

BODY PARAGRAPH 1 — Chapters 1–4: vision and military bravery (7 sentences — about 220 words)

Scene focus: Snowball codifies Animalism in Chapter 2; Snowball leads the defence at the Battle of the Cowshed in Chapter 4.

• Topic sentence: Orwell establishes Snowball as the revolution's visionary architect — the pig whose intellect codifies Animalism into the Seven Commandments and whose physical bravery at the Battle of the Cowshed earns him the medal "Animal Hero, First Class" — so that the later erasure of his record will register as historical falsification rather than mere political reversal.

• Technique + evidence: Orwell deploys foil pairing inside characterisation — Snowball described as "a more vivacious pig than Napoleon, quicker in speech and more inventive, but was not considered to have the same depth of character" (Chapter 2) — combining foil pairing (the parallel construction setting Snowball's eloquence against Napoleon's silent gravity, the syntax already preparing the reader for the contest the pigs will resolve) with free indirect discourse (the verdict "was not considered" presented as community judgement, the agentless grammar erasing the speaker so that the misjudgement seems collective rather than authored) inside the same sentence.

• Close analysis: The triplet "vivacious / quicker / inventive" names the qualities a debating revolution requires, while the qualifier "but was not considered" signals the verdict that will, by Chapter 5, be enforced by the dogs — the close-analysis grammar telling the reader, on second pass, that the community's misjudgement is the precondition of Napoleon's seizure.

• Effect on reader 1: Thus the reader feels admiration for Snowball's articulacy and unease at the verdict the syntax has already passed against him — recognising the gap between visible merit and political prospect that is the visionary's permanent condition.

• Effect on reader 2: Indeed, the reader perceives the foundational error of the early revolution — the elevation of Napoleon's "depth of character" over Snowball's measurable contributions — and registers Orwell's argument that revolutions misjudge the figures most likely to keep them honest.

• Author's purpose: Orwell may be arguing that the revolutionary intelligentsia's vulnerability lies precisely in its visibility — the figures who articulate the principles are the ones the strongman must displace first, because their continued presence would expose the betrayal in language the masses can recognise.

• Context (AO3): Trotsky led the Red Army during the Russian Civil War of 1918–22 and was Lenin's named co-leader of the October Revolution — yet Stalin, occupying the General Secretary's clerical chair, was already cultivating the bureaucratic loyalties that would, within five years of Lenin's death in January 1924, drive Trotsky from the Communist Party.

BODY PARAGRAPH 2 — Chapter 5: expulsion by the dogs (7 sentences — about 230 words)

Scene focus: Snowball presents the windmill plans; Napoleon urinates on them; the dogs drive Snowball off the farm.

• Topic sentence: Where Chapters 1–4 had installed Snowball as visionary, Chapter 5 stages the structural moment in which the visionary is removed — not through democratic defeat but through the dogs Napoleon had been training in secret since Chapter 3, the expulsion enacting Orwell's argument that the unaccountable revolution must remove its own most articulate defenders the moment they propose anything that threatens the strongman's authority.

• Technique + evidence: Orwell deploys symbolic action paired with auditory shock — Napoleon "walked heavily round the shed, looked closely at every detail of the plans and snuffed at them once or twice, then stood for a little while contemplating them out of the corner of his eye; then suddenly he lifted his leg, urinated over the plans, and walked out without uttering a word" (Chapter 5) — combining symbolic action (the urination on the windmill plans condensing the totalitarian rejection of intellect into a single bodily gesture) with cumulative syntax (the seven verbs "walked / looked / snuffed / stood / lifted / urinated / walked" enacting the deliberateness of the contempt within the sentence rhythm itself) inside the same paragraph.

• Close analysis: The verb "urinated" is grammatically passive in feeling — the act framed as bodily reflex rather than political choice — while the closing "without uttering a word" exposes the totalitarian rejection of debate, the silence the same silence Napoleon had wrapped around his accumulation of force from Chapter 2.

• Effect on reader 1: Thus the reader experiences the scene as both comic and disturbing — the laughter at the crude physicality curdling into recognition that this is the moment debate ends and force begins.

• Effect on reader 2: Indeed, the reader registers the structural pivot of the novella — that the regime which cannot defeat its rival's ideas degrades them physically, and that the dogs of the next page are the institutional form of the urination.

• Author's purpose: Orwell may be demonstrating that the visionary's expulsion is not an accident of personality clash but the inevitable logic of any revolution that refuses to specify accountability — the regime requires the visionary's silence because the visionary's voice is the only instrument by which the betrayal could be named.

• Context (AO3): Trotsky was expelled from the Communist Party in November 1927 after Stalin manoeuvred him into political isolation, exiled from the Soviet Union in 1929, and assassinated in Mexico City in August 1940 by Ramón Mercader, an NKVD agent wielding an ice-axe — the dogs of Chapter 5 are the historical NKVD condensed into the barn.

BODY PARAGRAPH 3 — Chapters 7–10: retroactive rewriting (7 sentences — about 230 words)

Scene focus: Squealer rewrites Snowball's record in Chapter 7; the sheep bleat "Snowball is in league with Jones"; the rewriting culminates in Chapter 8's official history.

• Topic sentence: Where Chapter 5 had expelled Snowball physically, Chapters 7–10 erase him historically — Squealer's pluperfect tense converting the visionary into the traitor, the sheep's bleating drowning out the few animals who remember the Battle of the Cowshed, and the narrative absence of any defence completing Orwell's claim that the totalitarian regime's most lethal weapon is not the dogs but the rewriting of the past.

• Technique + evidence: Orwell deploys pluperfect rewriting inside Squealer's anaphoric address — Squealer announces that Snowball "had seemed to oppose" the windmill "simply as a manoeuvre to get rid of Snowball" and that "the windmill was, in fact, Napoleon's own creation" (Chapter 5, retrospectively) — combining pluperfect rewriting (the grammatical instrument that converts what was into what had been, the tense rearranging chronology so that opposition becomes foresight) with the bureaucratic adverbs "in fact" and "simply" (the registers of false transparency that announce manipulation as plain truth) inside the same retrospective.

• Close analysis: The qualifier "in fact" performs the totalitarian register precisely because it admits no evidence — the reader, who watched Napoleon urinate on the plans in real time, experiences the gap between remembered reality and official narrative as a physical discomfort that mirrors the animals' own confusion.

• Effect on reader 1: Thus the reader feels cognitive vertigo — the past the previous chapter installed now overwritten by the chapter the reader is currently inside — the technique enacting the operation it is describing.

• Effect on reader 2: Indeed, the reader recognises that historical falsification is not a propaganda accessory but the regime's central instrument — the past must be rewritten because the present can only be defended if the alternative is erased.

• Author's purpose: Orwell may be arguing that the visionary's posthumous reputation is the regime's most dangerous remaining threat — and that the totalitarian instrument of choice is not the killing of the visionary but the rewriting of the visionary into traitor, so that even those who remember are taught to doubt their own memory.

• Context (AO3): Stalinist photographs of the early Bolsheviks were retouched across the 1930s to remove Trotsky, Kamenev and Zinoviev from the historical record — the practice meticulously documented by David King in The Commissar Vanishes (1997) — and Squealer's pluperfect rewriting of Snowball is the textual form of that visual erasure.

CONCLUSION (4 sentences — about 145 words)

• Restated thesis: In conclusion, Orwell traces Snowball's arc from visionary architect, through expulsion by the dogs, to retroactive rewriting into traitor — the structural casualty of any revolution that refuses to specify accountability.

• Controlling concept: What Chapter 2 installed as eloquence and bravery, Chapter 5 expelled through force, Chapters 7–10 erased through rewriting — three stages of the same elimination, the consistency of the operation confirming Orwell's diagnosis that the visionary is the figure the unaccountable regime must remove first.

• Author's purpose: Writing from his own Spanish experience of watching Stalinist forces suppress the POUM in Barcelona in 1937, Orwell embodies in Snowball the historical fate of the revolutionary intelligentsia — the figures the early revolution depended upon and the late revolution had to silence.

• Ultimate moral: Snowball's enduring force as a fable creation is that the reader cannot defend him because the text itself records his erasure — and the reader's discomfort at that erasure is Orwell's pedagogy, training the reader to recognise the operation when it is repeated under different names.

Q3 — Boxer

Question

How does Orwell use the character of Boxer to explore ideas about the working class in Animal Farm?

Write about:

- how Orwell presents Boxer - how Orwell uses Boxer to explore ideas about the working class.

[30 marks + 4 AO4 = 34 marks]

Frame

Boxer is the working class made visible — the cart-horse whose body builds the windmill, whose two maxims hold the regime together, and whose collapse in Chapter 9 is the controlling concept consummated in a single body. The plan tracks the three structural beats of allegorical tragedy — investment in Chapter 3, escalation across Chapters 5–8, consumption in Chapter 9.

Technique gloss:

• Allegorical naming — Boxer: the Chinese Boxer Rebellion (1899–1901), the working-class uprising the European powers crushed; the name signals labour given and given until it cannot.

• Maxim — short formulaic statement; Boxer's "I will work harder" (Chapter 3) and "Napoleon is always right" (Chapter 5).

• Free indirect discourse — Clover's silent recognition fused with the narrative voice in Chapter 9.

• Bathos — the deflation of the tragic into the commercial via the side of the knacker's van.

• Symbolic geography — pasture (labour), windmill (escalating labour), knacker's van (consumption).

• Foil pairing — Boxer against Benjamin: investment against cynicism; both survive the revolution, only one is destroyed by it.

INTRODUCTION (3 sentences — about 120 words)

• Hook (historical fact): Alexei Stakhanov, a coal-miner from the Donbas, was credited in August 1935 with mining 102 tonnes of coal in a single shift — fourteen times the official quota — and the Soviet propaganda apparatus converted his name into the Stakhanovite movement, an industrial cult of overwork that drove the Five-Year Plans and contributed to mass mortality across the workforce.

• Building sentence (historical context): Orwell, who had observed working-class loyalty consumed by an unaccountable leadership during his Spanish service in 1936–37, names his cart-horse Boxer for the Chinese Boxer Rebellion of 1899–1901 — the working-class uprising the European powers crushed — and constructs Boxer's two maxims ("I will work harder" and "Napoleon is always right") as the linguistic form of the loyalty that the regime requires and consumes.

• Thesis: Across his Chapter 3 investment in the new order through the maxim "I will work harder", his Chapters 5–8 escalation through the windmill labour and the second maxim "Napoleon is always right", and his Chapter 9 consumption when he is sold to "Alfred Simmonds, Horse Slaughterer and Glue Boiler" for the price of whisky for the pigs, Orwell presents Boxer as the working class consummated in a single body — loyal to the regime that requires his loyalty and is built to consume it.

BODY PARAGRAPH 1 — Chapter 3: investment (7 sentences — about 220 words)

Scene focus: the first harvest after the revolution; Boxer adopts the maxim "I will work harder".

• Topic sentence: Orwell installs Boxer's loyalty not as a personal virtue but as the structural beat of investment — the moment the working class commits to the new order, the maxim "I will work harder" offered as the answer to every problem the new regime will subsequently produce.

• Technique + evidence: Orwell deploys maxim inside characterisation — "His answer to every problem, every setback, was 'I will work harder!' — which he had adopted as his personal motto" (Chapter 3) — combining maxim (the formulaic statement that converts a complex political situation into a single behavioural reflex) with absolute syntax ("every problem, every setback", the two parallel universals leaving no situation unanswered, the reflex pre-applied to circumstances Boxer has not yet encountered) inside the same sentence.

• Close analysis: The possessive "his personal motto" converts a public political slogan into a private psychological commitment — the regime's investment requirement now lodged inside Boxer's own self-understanding — while the parallelism of "every problem, every setback" signals the cost: the maxim is pre-committed to circumstances the regime can engineer without limit.

• Effect on reader 1: Thus the reader feels admiration for Boxer's commitment and unease at the absoluteness of the formula — recognising that loyalty without conditional clauses is loyalty the regime can extract without limit.

• Effect on reader 2: Indeed, the reader perceives the controlling concept's investment beat — the working class committing to a new order whose accountability mechanisms have not been specified — and registers Orwell's argument that the loyalty is honourable and the structural conditions of its extraction are not.

• Author's purpose: Orwell may be arguing that the working class's investment is the revolution's most precious resource — and that any unaccountable leadership will, by structural logic, convert that investment into the labour the leadership consumes.

• Context (AO3): The Stakhanovite movement, named for Alexei Stakhanov's August 1935 coal-mining feat in the Donbas, became the Soviet Union's official cult of overwork during the Second Five-Year Plan (1933–37) — Soviet workers, like Boxer, supplied the labour that the Plans required and the regime that the Plans empowered did not return to them.

BODY PARAGRAPH 2 — Chapters 5–8: escalation (7 sentences — about 230 words)

Scene focus: the windmill labour across Chapters 6–8; the second maxim "Napoleon is always right" in Chapter 5; the Battle of the Windmill in Chapter 8.

• Topic sentence: Where Chapter 3 had installed Boxer's investment, Chapters 5–8 stage the escalation — the windmill consuming his body, the second maxim "Napoleon is always right" foreclosing his capacity to question the regime, and the Battle of the Windmill exposing the labour as the regime's instrument rather than the workers' achievement.

• Technique + evidence: Orwell deploys symbolic action inside the second maxim — "He had now had time to think things over. If Comrade Napoleon says it, it must be right" (Chapter 5) — combining symbolic action (Boxer's adoption of "Napoleon is always right" immediately after the dogs' expulsion of Snowball, the maxim taking the place of the political analysis the expulsion has foreclosed) with the conditional construction "if X says it, it must be" (the syntax converting authority into truth, the modal "must" eliminating the gap in which dissent could occur) inside the same passage.

• Close analysis: The verb phrase "had now had time to think things over" performs the arrival of the maxim as the conclusion of deliberation — yet the deliberation has produced not analysis but submission, the syntax exposing that the second maxim is not the result of thinking but the substitute for it.

• Effect on reader 1: Thus the reader feels the tightening of the regime's hold — Boxer's loyalty no longer answering individual problems but pre-resolving every future political question, the maxim consuming the cognitive space that critique would require.

• Effect on reader 2: Indeed, the reader registers the structural escalation — the regime now not only extracting Boxer's labour but the maxim that licenses every subsequent extraction, the loyalty engineered to outlast every betrayal.

• Author's purpose: Orwell may be demonstrating that the working class's consent to the regime is engineered through the simplification of complex political reality into reflex maxims — and that the maxims, once installed, function as the regime's most economical instrument of control.

• Context (AO3): The Soviet propaganda apparatus, including the newspaper Pravda (literally "Truth") founded in 1912 and Stalinised after 1924, manufactured precisely the maxims Boxer adopts — "Stalin is the Lenin of today" and similar formulations were the linguistic form of Boxer's "Napoleon is always right", the working class's loyalty converted into linguistic reflex.

BODY PARAGRAPH 3 — Chapter 9: consumption (7 sentences — about 235 words)

Scene focus: Boxer collapses pulling the stones for the windmill; he is sold to the knacker; Clover and Benjamin watch the van drive away.

• Topic sentence: Where Chapters 5–8 had escalated the extraction, Chapter 9 consummates it — Boxer's collapse under the windmill stones converting his body into the labour-cost the regime has been extracting since Chapter 3, and the side of the knacker's van condensing the controlling concept into a single proper noun.

• Technique + evidence: Orwell deploys bathetic specificity inside dramatic irony — "A large closed van, drawn by two horses, drove up to the farm… 'Alfred Simmonds, Horse Slaughterer and Glue Boiler, Willingdon. Dealer in Hides and Bone-Meal. Kennels Supplied'" (Chapter 9) — combining bathetic specificity (the commercial precision of the proper noun and the catalogue of trade titles deflating the tragic into the transactional) with dramatic irony (the reader recognises the van's purpose seconds before the animals do, the gap between recognition and recognition the structural form of the working class's tragedy) inside the same passage.

• Close analysis: The catalogue "Hides and Bone-Meal. Kennels Supplied" registers Boxer not as the cart-horse the reader has followed for nine chapters but as the raw material of a commercial transaction — the loyal worker now classified by the trades into which his body will be converted, the syntax enacting the conversion it is naming.

• Effect on reader 1: Thus the reader feels outrage that exceeds the allegorical frame — the betrayal of loyalty registering as a universal injustice the fable form has prepared the reader to recognise but not blunted.

• Effect on reader 2: Indeed, the reader perceives the structural beat of consumption arriving on schedule — the investment of Chapter 3 and the escalation of Chapters 5–8 having generated, by the inexorable logic of unaccountable revolution, the body now disappearing inside the closed van.

• Author's purpose: Orwell may be demonstrating that the working class's loyalty is not a moral achievement of the regime but a resource the regime extracts — and that the regime which has extracted the labour will, when the body is no longer productive, convert the body itself into the commodity that funds the regime's continuing comfort.

• Restated thesis: In conclusion, Orwell consummates the working class's allegorical tragedy in Boxer — investment in Chapter 3, escalation across Chapters 5–8, consumption in Chapter 9 — the three non-negotiable beats of the structure delivered with the precision that fable form demands.

• Controlling concept: What Chapter 3 installed as the maxim of investment, Chapters 5–8 escalated through windmill labour and second maxim, Chapter 9 consumed through the side of the closed van — three stages of the same exploitation, the working class's loyalty engineered for the regime's extraction at every beat.

• Author's purpose: Writing for a 1945 readership about to elect Attlee's Labour government on a platform of welfare and nationalisation, Orwell embodies in Boxer the warning that loyalty without accountability is the resource any regime will, by structural necessity, exhaust.

• Ultimate moral: Boxer's enduring force is that the reader's grief at his death is the reader's already-recognition of the controlling concept — the regime that consumed him is the regime any unaccountable leadership reproduces, and the reader who closes the chapter unable to forget the van has been taught the analytical move that protects every labour from every Napoleon.

Q4 — Old Major

Question

How does Orwell use the character of Old Major to explore ideas about revolution and vision in Animal Farm?

Write about:

- how Orwell presents Old Major - how Orwell uses Old Major to explore ideas about revolution and vision.

[30 marks + 4 AO4 = 34 marks]

Frame

Old Major is the visionary diagnosis whose hamartia is the originating cause of everything that follows — the failure to specify, before the revolution, the mechanisms by which post-revolutionary leadership would be held accountable. The plan tracks three stages — Chapter 1 the diagnosis articulated and the anthem taught, the immediate aftermath of his death, the gap his absence leaves that the pigs occupy.

Technique gloss:

• Hamartia — error in action (Aristotle, Poetics); Old Major's failure to specify accountability mechanisms is the hamartia the rest of the novella enacts.

• Visionary speech — Major's Chapter 1 address as the textbook case of an announced controlling concept.

• Anaphora — repetition at the start of clauses ("Man is the only…").

• Tricolon — three escalating items ("All men are enemies. All animals are comrades" expanded in the address).

• Anthem / collective song — "Beasts of England" as the founding emotional expression of the revolution.

• Allegorical naming — Marx (the diagnosis) + Lenin (the call to action), with the title "Major" signalling military-pedagogical authority.

INTRODUCTION (3 sentences — about 115 words)

• Hook (historical fact): Karl Marx published The Communist Manifesto in February 1848, diagnosing capitalism as the exploitation of the working class by the owners of capital; Vladimir Lenin returned to Russia in April 1917 from exile in Zurich, his arrival converting the diagnosis into a call to action that the October Revolution then attempted to enact.

• Building sentence (historical context): Orwell's Old Major condenses both figures into a single boar — Marx for the diagnosis articulated in the Chapter 1 address, Lenin for the anthem and the call to action — and his death three nights later, in advance of the revolution itself, is the structural parallel to Lenin's death in January 1924 that left the Soviet succession open to a leadership Marx had not anticipated.

• Thesis: Across his Chapter 1 articulation of the diagnosis in the address to the assembled animals, his teaching of "Beasts of England" as the revolution's founding emotional expression, and the structural absence of any accountability mechanism in his vision that the pigs subsequently occupy, Orwell presents Old Major as the visionary whose diagnosis is honourable and whose hamartia — the failure to specify how power would be checked once the original visionary left the stage — is the originating cause of the betrayal that consumes the rest of the novella.

BODY PARAGRAPH 1 — Chapter 1: the diagnosis articulated (7 sentences — about 230 words)

Scene focus: Old Major addresses the assembled animals in the big barn by candlelight.

• Topic sentence: Orwell installs Old Major's diagnosis as the moral baseline of the novella — the articulated case for revolution against which every subsequent betrayal will be measured — the address itself the textbook case of an announced controlling concept that the rest of the text will dramatise.

• Technique + evidence: Orwell deploys anaphora inside catalogued accusation — Old Major's "Man is the only creature that consumes without producing. He does not give milk, he does not lay eggs, he is too weak to pull the plough, he cannot run fast enough to catch rabbits. Yet he is lord of all the animals" (Chapter 1) — combining anaphora (the repeated "He does not… he does not… he is too weak… he cannot" sequence cataloguing human incapacity through grammatical accumulation) with antithesis (the catalogue of incapacity set against the closing "Yet he is lord of all the animals", the contradiction supplying the revolutionary case in a single rhetorical step) inside the same passage.

• Close analysis: The pronoun "he" repeated through the catalogue refuses Man the dignity of personhood, returning the linguistic violence Man has performed on the animals back through the syntax — while the conjunction "Yet" names the structural injustice in a single monosyllabic word, the smallness of the conjunction matched only by the size of the case it carries.

• Effect on reader 1: Thus the reader hears the case for revolution articulated with the lucid force of authentic political analysis — the speech persuasive in itself, the persuasiveness essential to the controlling concept Orwell is defending.

• Effect on reader 2: Indeed, the reader registers Orwell's most precise structural choice — the diagnosis is honourable, and the betrayal that follows is therefore not the refutation of the original case but the warning that the case must be defended against the leadership it produces.

• Author's purpose: Orwell may be insisting through Major's address that anti-Stalinism and anti-socialism are not the same position — that the diagnosis Marx published in 1848 retains its force regardless of the betrayal Stalin enacted, and that the reader who confuses the two has misread both the speech and the novella.

• Context (AO3): Karl Marx's The Communist Manifesto (February 1848) opens with "a spectre is haunting Europe" and proceeds through a structural analysis of capitalist exploitation — the address Old Major delivers in Chapter 1 condenses the Manifesto's diagnostic move into a single page, and the Sophicly framing of Orwell as "democratic socialist" (his own self-description in "Why I Write", 1946) confirms the diagnosis is not what Orwell is satirising.

BODY PARAGRAPH 2 — Chapter 1 closing: the anthem and the gap (7 sentences — about 230 words)

Scene focus: Old Major teaches "Beasts of England"; the animals sing it five times before sleep.

• Topic sentence: Where the address had installed the diagnosis, the closing of Chapter 1 installs the emotional form — "Beasts of England" as the revolution's founding song, the collective singing the moment the diagnosis becomes shared political feeling, and the gap the song does not address (what happens to the leadership after the revolution) the structural omission Orwell will spend the next nine chapters dramatising.

• Technique + evidence: Orwell deploys collective ritual paired with prophetic register — Old Major teaches "Beasts of England" and "the whole farm burst out into Beasts of England in tremendous unison… they sang it right through five times in succession" (Chapter 1) — combining collective ritual (the five-fold singing converting the diagnosis into shared political identity) with prophetic register (the lyric "Tyrant Man shall be o'erthrown / And the fruitful fields of England / Shall be trod by beasts alone", the future tense announcing the revolution as inevitable) inside the same scene.

• Close analysis: The intensifier "tremendous unison" names the achievement of collective political feeling — the rare moment when the diagnosis and the masses align — while the ordinal "five times in succession" registers the depth of the commitment, the repetition both the strength of the moment and the absence of any accompanying institutional design.

• Effect on reader 1: Thus the reader feels the warmth of the collective revolutionary moment and the foreboding of its fragility — the song's emotional completeness only sharpening the recognition that the song specifies no mechanism by which the leadership it celebrates will be checked.

• Effect on reader 2: Indeed, the reader perceives Old Major's hamartia not as personal failure but as the structural omission Orwell will diagnose retrospectively — the visionary's articulation of what the revolution must overthrow lacking the equally specific articulation of how the post-revolutionary leadership will be prevented from reproducing what was overthrown.

• Author's purpose: Orwell may be arguing that the visionary's job is not merely to diagnose the disease but to specify, in advance, the prophylactic — and that any vision that names the enemy without naming the accountability mechanism is the precondition of the regime that subsequently betrays the vision.

• Context (AO3): Lenin's State and Revolution (1917) attempted to specify post-revolutionary structures but its democratic-centralism framework was, by Lenin's own death in January 1924, already converging on the bureaucratic centralism Stalin would weaponise — the gap Orwell dramatises in Old Major's address is the historical gap State and Revolution failed to close.

BODY PARAGRAPH 3 — the gap occupied (7 sentences — about 230 words)

Scene focus: Old Major dies three nights after his address; the pigs immediately organise the principles into Animalism; the milk and apples are appropriated within hours of the revolution.

• Topic sentence: Where the address and the anthem had installed the diagnosis and the feeling, Old Major's death three nights later opens the structural gap the rest of the novella enacts — the pigs' immediate organisation of Animalism, the appropriation of the milk and apples within hours of the revolution, and the eventual displacement of the diagnosis by Squealer's propaganda all consequences of the gap the visionary did not close.

• Technique + evidence: Orwell deploys structural absence inside narrative compression — "Three nights later old Major died peacefully in his sleep. His body was buried at the foot of the orchard" (Chapter 1) — combining structural absence (the death narrated in two short sentences, the visionary removed from the text the moment the diagnosis is delivered) with bathetic understatement (the verb "buried" and the prepositional phrase "at the foot of the orchard" registering the death without ceremony, the structural cause delivered with the lucid economy Orwell's prose requires) inside the same passage.

• Close analysis: The temporal marker "Three nights later" compresses the gap between vision and execution into a span the reader can hold — and the brevity itself enacts the structural problem: the visionary's absence arrives so quickly after the vision that no accountability framework has been installed to outlive him.

• Effect on reader 1: Thus the reader experiences the death not as personal grief but as structural exposure — the foundation laid in Chapter 1 already withdrawn by Chapter 2, the rest of the building therefore at the mercy of whoever subsequently occupies the site.

• Effect on reader 2: Indeed, the reader registers the recurring totalitarian pattern — the visionary's death as the precondition of the strongman's seizure, the absence of the visionary as the silence the regime fills with its own voice.

• Author's purpose: Orwell may be demonstrating that the original revolutionary case is honourable and that the original revolutionary visionary is honourable, and that the structural failing — the unspecified accountability mechanism — is the disease that recurs across regimes regardless of the visionary's personal merit.

• Context (AO3): Lenin died on 21 January 1924 after a series of strokes; his Testament, dictated in late 1922 and early 1923, warned against Stalin's accumulation of bureaucratic power, but the document was suppressed by the Politburo for thirty-three years — the gap between Major's address and Major's death is the historical gap between Lenin's diagnosis and Lenin's silence.

CONCLUSION (4 sentences — about 145 words)

• Restated thesis: In conclusion, Orwell constructs Old Major as the honourable diagnosis whose hamartia is the originating structural cause — Chapter 1 articulating the case, the anthem installing the feeling, the death opening the gap that the pigs occupy.

• Controlling concept: What the address installed as the diagnosis, the anthem installed as collective feeling, the death exposed as structural omission — three stages of the same vision, the consistency confirming Orwell's claim that any revolution that ignores accountability ends as the tyranny it overthrew.

• Author's purpose: Writing as a self-described democratic socialist for a 1945 readership about to enter the Cold War, Orwell uses Major's hamartia to insist that the diagnosis must outlive the visionary — that the next vision must specify, in advance, the mechanism by which the leadership it produces will be held to account.

• Ultimate moral: Old Major's enduring force is that the diagnosis he articulates remains, after ten chapters of betrayal, the only accurate description of the regime that has destroyed it — and Orwell's pedagogy is to send the reader back to the address with the analytical equipment to defend the diagnosis from the regime that has captured its language.

Q5 — Squealer

Question

How does Orwell use the character of Squealer to explore ideas about propaganda and language in Animal Farm?

Write about:

- what Squealer says and does - how Orwell uses Squealer to convey his ideas about propaganda and language.

[30 marks + 4 AO4 = 34 marks]

Frame

Squealer is the propaganda apparatus given a single body — the rhetorical instrument by which every betrayal of the revolution is processed into the language of continuity, every appropriation justified, every commandment rewritten. The plan tracks three stages — Chapter 3's first appropriation (the milk and apples), Chapter 5's rewriting of Snowball, Chapter 9's euphemism over the knacker's van.

Technique gloss:

• Allegorical naming — Squealer: pigs squeal; the verb means both to vocalise loudly and to inform on others; the name signals the propaganda voice and the secret-police function.

• Pluperfect tense — "had seemed", "had been" — the grammatical instrument of historical falsification.

• Euphemism — "readjustment" (Chapter 9 work-rations), "the necessary surgical operation" (Chapter 7 register).

• Sycophantic register — "Surely, comrades, you would not want Jones back?" (Chapter 5 onward) — the rhetorical question that converts dissent into nostalgia for tyranny.

• Capacity for inversion — "He could turn black into white" (Chapter 2) — the gatekeeping function named directly.

• Allegorical mapping — Squealer = Pravda, the official Soviet newspaper (literally "Truth").

INTRODUCTION (3 sentences — about 120 words)

• Hook (historical fact): Pravda — literally "Truth" — was founded in St Petersburg in May 1912 as a Bolshevik newspaper and became, after 1924, the official organ of the Soviet Communist Party, where editors rewrote the historical record across the 1930s to remove Trotsky, Kamenev and Zinoviev from photographs, manufacture confessions for the Moscow show trials, and convert every famine, every purge, every diplomatic reversal into evidence of revolutionary success.

• Building sentence (historical context): Orwell argued in "Politics and the English Language" (April 1946) that vague, abstract, euphemistic prose is the linguistic instrument of totalitarianism — and Squealer, the "brilliant talker" (Chapter 2) whose voice carries every betrayal of the revolution through a wall of bureaucratic vocabulary, is the textual case the essay's diagnosis predicts.

• Thesis: Across his Chapter 3 first justification of the milk and apples appropriation, his Chapter 5 retroactive rewriting of Snowball into Jones's secret agent, and his Chapter 9 euphemism over the knacker's van that conceals Boxer's slaughter, Orwell presents Squealer as the propaganda apparatus made flesh — the rhetorical instrument that converts every betrayal of the revolution into the language of continuity and ensures the gap between the regime's actions and the regime's vocabulary remains permanently closed.

BODY PARAGRAPH 1 — Chapter 3: first appropriation (7 sentences — about 230 words)

Scene focus: the pigs appropriate the milk and apples within hours of the revolution; Squealer arrives to explain.

• Topic sentence: Orwell installs Squealer's propaganda function not as accessory to the regime but as foundational — the moment of the first appropriation requiring, immediately, the rhetorical instrument that will license every subsequent appropriation, the propaganda apparatus running ahead of the betrayals it justifies.

• Technique + evidence: Orwell deploys sycophantic register inside rhetorical question — Squealer's "Comrades! You do not imagine, I hope, that we pigs are doing this in a spirit of selfishness and privilege? Many of us actually dislike milk and apples… It is for your sake that we drink that milk and eat those apples" (Chapter 3) — combining sycophantic register (the address "Comrades!" invoking revolutionary solidarity in the act of dissolving it) with rhetorical question ("You do not imagine, I hope", the negative construction pre-emptively shaming the dissent the question is engineered to suppress) inside the same address.

• Close analysis: The verb "dislike" registers the rhetorical inversion the technique requires — the regime's appropriation framed as sacrifice on behalf of the appropriated — while the closing "It is for your sake" converts theft into benevolence, the syntax enacting the moral inversion the propaganda is performing.

• Effect on reader 1: Thus the reader hears the inversion and feels its rhetorical effectiveness — recognising that the absurdity of the claim is precisely what makes the technique successful, the audacity of the lie pre-empting the question the listener might otherwise ask.

• Effect on reader 2: Indeed, the reader perceives the foundational role of propaganda in the regime — that the milk and apples episode in Chapter 3 is not a marginal moment but the prototype the propaganda apparatus will replicate for every subsequent appropriation across the next seven chapters.

• Author's purpose: Orwell may be demonstrating that the totalitarian regime requires its propaganda apparatus operational from the first betrayal onward — that any appropriation will, within hours, be processed by the rhetorical instrument that converts the appropriation into evidence of the regime's continuing virtue.

• Context (AO3): Pravda, the official Soviet newspaper founded in 1912 and Stalinised after 1924, ran precisely the rhetorical inversions Squealer performs — Stalin's appropriations of luxury were processed as sacrifices on behalf of the proletariat, the famines of the early 1930s presented as the necessary sacrifice for the Five-Year Plans, the rhetorical machinery running in parallel to the appropriations it justified.

BODY PARAGRAPH 2 — Chapter 5: rewriting Snowball (7 sentences — about 230 words)

Scene focus: Squealer rewrites Snowball's role at the Battle of the Cowshed; the dogs stand behind him.

• Topic sentence: Where Chapter 3 had installed the propaganda function, Chapter 5 reveals its second operation — the rewriting of the historical record, the conversion of Snowball's bravery at the Battle of the Cowshed into Jones's secret agency, the propaganda apparatus now converting not only present appropriation but past evidence into the regime's preferred narrative.

• Technique + evidence: Orwell deploys pluperfect rewriting paired with the silent threat of the dogs — Squealer announces that the windmill "had seemed to oppose" was "in fact, Napoleon's own creation" and "this very morning had been Snowball's secret agreement with Jones" (Chapter 5) — combining pluperfect rewriting (the tense "had seemed", "had been" converting what was into what had been, the grammatical instrument of historical falsification) with the silent threat of the dogs (the propaganda licensed by the violence that stands beside the speaker, the rhetorical authority underwritten by the physical authority no rhetoric is required to invoke) inside the same address.

• Close analysis: The qualifier "in fact" performs the totalitarian register — the adverb that admits no evidence — while the temporal marker "this very morning" registers the velocity of the rewriting, the past converted into the propaganda's preferred shape within the same news cycle that the dogs have just enforced.

• Effect on reader 1: Thus the reader experiences the cognitive dissonance the technique is engineered to produce — the past the previous chapter installed now overwritten by the chapter the reader is currently inside, the discomfort the same discomfort the animals' silence registers.

• Effect on reader 2: Indeed, the reader registers Orwell's most precise structural claim — that the propaganda apparatus is not the regime's accessory but its central instrument, and that the rewriting of Snowball is not an isolated lie but the prototype of the regime's relationship with the historical record.

• Author's purpose: Orwell may be arguing that the propaganda apparatus is the structural complement to the dogs — the rhetorical instrument that converts the violence into ideology, the speech that licenses the silence the dogs enforce.

• Context (AO3): Stalinist photographs of the early Bolsheviks were retouched across the 1930s to remove Trotsky, Kamenev and Zinoviev from the historical record — David King's The Commissar Vanishes (1997) documents the pattern in detail — and Squealer's pluperfect rewriting of Snowball is the textual form of that visual erasure.

Scene focus: Boxer's collapse and sale to the knacker; Squealer arrives to "correct" the rumour.

• Topic sentence: Where Chapter 5 had revealed the rewriting of the historical record, Chapter 9 stages the propaganda apparatus at its most cynical — the euphemism deployed not to correct a misunderstanding but to launder a transaction, the lie about the veterinary surgeon converting Boxer's slaughter into the regime's official act of compassion.

• Close analysis: The pluperfect "had been bought" and "had not yet painted" perform the same rewriting Squealer used on Snowball — the past tense converting present transaction into completed bureaucratic procedure — while the closing "yet" does the heavy work, registering the lie as a temporary administrative oversight rather than an evidence of the slaughter the reader has watched.

• Effect on reader 1: Thus the reader feels the most disturbing of all the propaganda episodes — the lie deployed not against present action but against the working-class body that the regime has just consumed, the rhetorical instrument arriving to launder the transaction whose victim the reader has just grieved.

• Effect on reader 2: Indeed, the reader recognises that the propaganda apparatus has now consumed even Boxer's death — that the regime requires the lie not because the animals would otherwise rebel but because the historical record must, even at the moment of the working-class body's disappearance, conform to the regime's narrative.

• Author's purpose: Orwell may be demonstrating that the totalitarian regime extracts not only the working class's labour and life but the working class's death itself — converting even the moment of consumption into evidence of the regime's continuing benevolence, the propaganda apparatus arriving last to ensure that even the grief is the regime's property.

• Context (AO3): Soviet propaganda across the 1930s and 1940s consistently announced the deaths of workers in industrial accidents, gulag executions and Ukrainian famine as the regime's continuing care for the proletariat — the pattern of euphemism Squealer deploys over the knacker's van is the linguistic operation Orwell would diagnose at length in Nineteen Eighty-Four (1949) and which the essay "Politics and the English Language" (April 1946) anatomises as the totalitarian use of vague abstract prose.

CONCLUSION (4 sentences — about 145 words)

• Restated thesis: In conclusion, Orwell constructs Squealer as the propaganda apparatus made flesh — Chapter 3 installing the foundational rhetorical operation, Chapter 5 escalating into historical rewriting, Chapter 9 deploying the euphemism over the working class's death itself.

• Controlling concept: What Chapter 3 installed as appropriation justification, Chapter 5 escalated as historical falsification, Chapter 9 cynically extended over Boxer's body — three operations of the same propaganda apparatus, each more lethal than the last.

• Author's purpose: Writing the same year he was preparing the essay "Politics and the English Language" (April 1946), Orwell uses Squealer to demonstrate, at the level of fictional incident, the linguistic operation the essay would diagnose at the level of analysis — the conversion of clear thought into vague abstraction as the precondition of every totalitarian regime.

• Ultimate moral: Squealer's enduring force is that the reader's laughter at his rhetoric is the reader's recognition of the operation — the audacity of the inversions training the reader to defend the language by which power can be named accurately, which is the analytical move the essay names as the citizen's defence against the regime.

Q6 — Power and Corruption

Question

How does Orwell explore ideas about power and corruption in Animal Farm?

Write about:

- what different characters do - how Orwell presents ideas about power and corruption.

[30 marks + 4 AO4 = 34 marks]

Frame

Power and corruption are the novella's central political diagnosis — installed by the milk-and-apples appropriation within hours of the revolution, escalated through the dogs and the rewriting of the commandments, and consummated in the closing dinner where the pigs and humans become indistinguishable. The plan tracks three stages — the first appropriation, the institutional seizure, the final indistinguishability.

Technique gloss:

• Cyclical structure — Manor Farm renamed Animal Farm renamed Manor Farm; the form is the corruption.

• Symbolic appropriation — milk and apples (Chapter 3); the farmhouse beds (Chapter 6); the whisky (Chapters 8–10).

• Auditory escalation — the "baying" of the dogs; the bleating of the sheep; the singing replaced by silence.

• Allegorical compression — the Five-Year Plans condensed into the windmill; the Great Terror condensed into the public executions.

• Visual climax — the pigs walking on two legs; the whip in the trotter (Chapter 10).

• Foil pairing — the principles announced (Chapter 2) against the principles rewritten (Chapter 10).

INTRODUCTION (3 sentences — about 120 words)

• Hook (historical fact): "Power tends to corrupt, and absolute power corrupts absolutely" — Lord Acton wrote those words in a private letter in April 1887, and Orwell, who first encountered the formulation as a schoolboy, used the diagnosis as the spine of his political writing from Homage to Catalonia (1938) through Animal Farm (1945) to Nineteen Eighty-Four (1949).

• Building sentence (historical context): Orwell wrote Animal Farm between November 1943 and February 1944 while the Soviet Union, then Britain's wartime ally, was being celebrated in British public discourse as a model of revolutionary success — and the novella was rejected by Faber, Gollancz and Cape on those grounds before Secker & Warburg published it in August 1945, the timing of the publication ensuring the diagnosis arrived as the Cold War was opening rather than as the Second World War was closing.

• Thesis: Across the Chapter 3 milk-and-apples appropriation in which the corruption begins within hours of the revolution, the Chapter 5–7 institutional seizure in which the dogs replace debate and the commandments are rewritten to license every transgression, and the Chapter 10 closing dinner in which the pigs and humans become indistinguishable, Orwell dramatises power and corruption as the structural disease that recurs across regimes — power without accountability the bacillus, the rewriting of the commandments the symptom, the closing image of pig and man the prognosis.

BODY PARAGRAPH 1 — Chapter 3: first appropriation (7 sentences — about 220 words)

Scene focus: the pigs take the milk and apples for themselves within hours of the revolution; Squealer arrives to explain.

• Topic sentence: Orwell installs power's corruption not as the revolution's gradual decay but as its immediate consequence — the milk-and-apples appropriation occurring within hours of the revolution itself, the corruption arriving so early that the principle of the revolution and the betrayal of the principle share the same chapter.

• Technique + evidence: Orwell deploys narrative compression inside ironic juxtaposition — "It had also been agreed that nobody else must come near it. So the milk was given to the pigs" (Chapter 3) — combining narrative compression (the appropriation arriving in a single short sentence, the brevity itself enacting the velocity of the corruption) with ironic juxtaposition (the verb "agreed" in the previous clause, the agreement engineered by the pigs whose appropriation the agreement licenses) inside the same paragraph.

• Close analysis: The passive "was given" erases agency — the milk given to no specified giver — while the modal "must" in "must come near it" registers the pre-emptive prohibition that protects the appropriation, the syntax confirming that the corruption requires its own protective vocabulary from the moment of its first occurrence.

• Effect on reader 1: Thus the reader feels the velocity of the betrayal — the corruption arriving so early that the reader is still recovering from the revolution itself — recognising that the gap between the principle and the practice is not the slow erosion of years but the immediate consequence of the revolution's failure to specify accountability.

• Effect on reader 2: Indeed, the reader registers Orwell's most precise structural claim — that the milk and apples episode is not a marginal moment but the prototype of every subsequent appropriation, the corruption a single bacillus seeded in the revolution's first hours and replicated across the rest of the chapters.

• Author's purpose: Orwell may be arguing that the revolution's failure to specify accountability mechanisms is the originating cause — that the pigs would not have taken the milk and apples if the institutional structures had been in place to prevent it, and that the corruption is therefore not personal vice but the structural consequence of the gap Old Major's vision left.

• Context (AO3): The Bolshevik leadership, within months of the October 1917 revolution, was already consolidating its own privileges — the nomenklatura system, established under Lenin and entrenched under Stalin, gave Party officials access to special shops, dachas and medical care that the general population did not — and the milk and apples episode is the institutional pattern condensed into a single morning.

BODY PARAGRAPH 2 — Chapters 5–7: institutional seizure (7 sentences — about 235 words)

Scene focus: the dogs drive Snowball off the farm; the Sunday meetings are abolished; the commandments are rewritten.

• Topic sentence: Where Chapter 3 had installed the corruption as immediate appropriation, Chapters 5–7 stage its institutionalisation — the dogs replacing debate, the Sunday meetings abolished, the commandments rewritten one by one — the corruption now no longer the regime's behaviour but the regime's permanent constitution.

• Technique + evidence: Orwell deploys symbolic action paired with passive announcement — "He announced that from now on the Sunday-morning Meetings would come to an end… All questions relating to the working of the farm would be settled by a special committee of pigs, presided over by himself" (Chapter 5) — combining symbolic action (the abolition of the Sunday meetings ending the only forum in which the principles could be defended through discussion) with passive announcement (the bureaucratic register "would come to an end", the agentless grammar disguising the seizure as administrative reform) inside the same announcement.

• Close analysis: The phrase "presided over by himself" exposes the seizure precisely because it states what the announcement was engineered to conceal — Napoleon's personal authority installed inside the language of collective governance, the close-analysis grammar registering the corruption that the announcement's vocabulary is performing.

• Effect on reader 1: Thus the reader feels the velocity of the institutional seizure — the corruption no longer requiring justification because the institution that would require justification has just been abolished.

• Effect on reader 2: Indeed, the reader recognises that the structural form of the corruption is the abolition of the institutions through which it could be challenged — that the totalitarian regime's first administrative move is to remove the procedural mechanisms that would allow accountability to operate.

• Author's purpose: Orwell may be demonstrating that power's corruption is not maintained through ongoing terror alone but through the abolition of the institutions of accountability — that the regime requires not only the dogs but the closure of the meetings, not only the executions but the rewriting of the commandments, and that each of these moves is the corruption made structural rather than personal.

• Context (AO3): The Soviet Union's transition from revolutionary councils (soviets) to one-party rule was effectively complete by 1921 with the banning of factions within the Communist Party at the Tenth Party Congress; by the late 1920s the Central Committee was a rubber stamp for Stalin's personal decisions, and the abolition of the Sunday meetings in Animal Farm condenses the institutional pattern into a single chapter.

BODY PARAGRAPH 3 — Chapter 10: indistinguishability (7 sentences — about 235 words)

Scene focus: the pigs walk on two legs; the closing dinner with Pilkington; the final image of pig and man.

• Topic sentence: Where Chapters 5–7 had institutionalised the corruption, Chapter 10 consummates it — the pigs walking on two legs, the closing dinner with Pilkington enacting the alliance with the very humans the revolution was meant to overthrow, and the final image of pig and man indistinguishable announcing that the corruption's ultimate achievement is the regime's indistinguishability from the regime it replaced.

• Technique + evidence: Orwell deploys syntactic mirroring inside cyclical closure — "The creatures outside looked from pig to man, and from man to pig, and from pig to man again; but already it was impossible to say which was which" (Chapter 10) — combining syntactic mirroring (the parallel construction "from pig to man, and from man to pig, and from pig to man again" enacting perceptual confusion within the sentence rhythm itself, the categories dissolving in the act of reading) with cyclical closure (the ending returning to the dynamic the revolution was launched to overthrow, the form announcing that the cycle has completed) inside the same final sentence.

• Close analysis: The adverb "already" compresses the entire ten-chapter trajectory into inevitability — the corruption named as having occurred in the past, before the watching animals could have named it — while the passive "it was impossible" strips the watching animals of agency, the syntax enacting the precise condition the controlling concept names: the corruption complete, the watching impossible to convert into action.

• Effect on reader 1: Thus the reader feels the closing image as a permanent residue — the indistinguishability lodging in the reader's mind beyond the text, the warning carried into every subsequent regime the reader will read about.

• Effect on reader 2: Indeed, the reader registers Orwell's most precise pedagogical move — the corruption named not as accident but as the inevitable consequence of unaccountable power, the closing image therefore not the failure of the revolution but the warning that the operation must be recognised before the next regime reproduces it.

• Author's purpose: Orwell may be demonstrating that the corruption's most lethal achievement is the indistinguishability — that the regime which has captured the revolution's vocabulary is the regime that the citizens of the subsequent generation cannot resist because they cannot name it as different from what it replaced.

• Context (AO3): The November 1943 Tehran Conference saw Stalin received as co-architect of the post-war order alongside Churchill and Roosevelt; by the August 1945 publication of Animal Farm, Soviet leaders had become indistinguishable from the Tsarist aristocracy in their use of palaces, secret police and personal cult — Orwell's closing image is the historical record condensed into a single sentence.

CONCLUSION (4 sentences — about 150 words)

• Restated thesis: In conclusion, Orwell anatomises power and corruption as the structural disease that recurs across regimes — Chapter 3 installing the first appropriation, Chapters 5–7 institutionalising the seizure, Chapter 10 consummating the indistinguishability.

• Controlling concept: What Chapter 3 installed as the immediate consequence of unaccountable power, Chapters 5–7 institutionalised through the abolition of the meetings and the rewriting of the commandments, Chapter 10 consummated through the closing image — three stages of the same diagnosis, the consistency confirming that the corruption is not personal vice but the inevitable consequence of any leadership not subject to accountability.

• Author's purpose: Writing for a readership about to enter the Cold War, Orwell uses the corruption arc to argue that anti-Stalinism is not anti-socialism — that the diagnosis must be applied to every leadership in every era, regardless of the vocabulary in which the leadership presents itself.

• Ultimate moral: Power's corruption is the bacillus the revolution must, in advance of seizing power, specify the prophylactic against — and the reader who closes the novella with the closing image lodged in memory has been taught the analytical move that, applied to the next leadership, may close the gap Old Major's vision did not.
