Q7 — Propaganda and Language

Question

How does Orwell explore ideas about propaganda and the manipulation of language in Animal Farm?

Write about:

- how language is used by different characters - how Orwell presents ideas about propaganda and the manipulation of language.

[30 marks + 4 AO4 = 34 marks]

Frame

Propaganda and language are the novella's primary instrument of corruption — the rewriting of the commandments, the simplification of the slogans, the replacement of the anthem, the euphemism that conceals the slaughter. The plan tracks three stages — the simplification (Chapter 3), the rewriting (Chapters 6–8), the cult (Chapters 8–10).

Technique gloss:

• Simplification of complex truth — "Four legs good, two legs bad" (Chapter 3) reducing the Seven Commandments to a single bleat.

• Pluperfect rewriting — "had seemed", "had been"; the grammatical instrument of historical falsification.

• Euphemism — "readjustment", "the necessary surgical operation", the lie about the veterinary surgeon.

• Sycophantic register — Squealer's "Surely, comrades, you would not want Jones back?" converting dissent into nostalgia for tyranny.

• Replacement anthem — "Beasts of England" (Chapter 1) abolished in Chapter 7 and replaced by "Comrade Napoleon" in Chapter 8.

• Choric repetition — the sheep's bleating drowning out dissent at every critical political moment.

INTRODUCTION (3 sentences — about 120 words)

• Hook (historical fact): Orwell published "Politics and the English Language" in Horizon magazine in April 1946 — the year after Animal Farm — and the essay's central argument was that the slovenly abstract prose of mid-twentieth-century political writing was the linguistic instrument by which totalitarianism converted clear thought into vague reflex; the diagnosis the essay analyses is the operation the novella has already dramatised.

• Building sentence (historical context): Pravda, the Soviet Communist Party's official newspaper founded in 1912 and Stalinised after 1924, had by the mid-1930s perfected the rhetorical operations Squealer deploys — the rewriting of the historical record, the manufacture of confessions for the Moscow show trials, the conversion of every famine and purge into evidence of revolutionary success.

• Thesis: Across the Chapter 3 simplification of the Seven Commandments into the sheep's "Four legs good, two legs bad", the Chapters 6–8 rewriting of the commandments to license every transgression of the original principles, and the Chapters 8–10 personality cult and replacement anthem that complete the regime's linguistic seizure of the revolution's vocabulary, Orwell presents propaganda and the manipulation of language as the totalitarian regime's most economical instrument — the rhetorical operation that converts every betrayal into the language of continuity and ensures the gap between regime action and regime vocabulary remains permanently closed.

BODY PARAGRAPH 1 — Chapter 3: simplification (7 sentences — about 220 words)

Scene focus: the Seven Commandments are simplified for the sheep to "Four legs good, two legs bad".

• Topic sentence: Orwell installs the regime's linguistic operation not as deception alone but as simplification — the conversion of the Seven Commandments into the sheep's single bleat, the political analysis of Animalism reduced to a reflex maxim that drowns out dissent at every subsequent critical moment.

• Technique + evidence: Orwell deploys choric repetition inside reductive paraphrase — "the sheep developed a great liking for this maxim, and often as they lay in the field they would all start bleating 'Four legs good, two legs bad! Four legs good, two legs bad!' and keep it up for hours on end, never growing tired of it" (Chapter 3) — combining choric repetition (the bleating that drowns dissent at every critical moment, the chorus inverted from moral commentary into propaganda) with reductive paraphrase (the seven commandments compressed into a four-word bleat, the simplification eliminating the cognitive space in which the original principles could be analysed) inside the same paragraph.

• Close analysis: The verb "never growing tired" registers the bleating as the propagandised mass's permanent condition — the chorus replaceable by no other utterance — while the temporal marker "for hours on end" signals the displacement of all alternative speech, the bleating consuming the silence in which dissent would otherwise occur.

• Effect on reader 1: Thus the reader hears the bleating as the auditory form of the controlling concept's first symptom — the simplification of complex political truth into a reflex incapable of distinguishing the regime from the principles the regime has betrayed.

• Effect on reader 2: Indeed, the reader recognises that the propaganda's first operation is not the explicit lie but the prior simplification — the masses must be taught the reflex before the lie can be installed inside it.

• Author's purpose: Orwell may be arguing that the propagandist's first job is not to deceive but to simplify — that vague, abstract, reflex-ready language is the precondition of every subsequent lie, and that the citizens of any regime must defend complex thought as the prophylactic against the propaganda the regime requires.

• Context (AO3): Orwell's essay "Politics and the English Language" (April 1946), written the year after Animal Farm, anatomises the same operation — slovenly abstract prose as the linguistic instrument of totalitarianism — and identifies the simplification of complex truth as the regime's most economical instrument of control.

BODY PARAGRAPH 2 — Chapters 6–8: rewriting (7 sentences — about 235 words)

Scene focus: the commandments are rewritten to add "with sheets" (Chapter 6); "to excess" (Chapter 8); "without cause" (Chapter 8).

• Topic sentence: Where Chapter 3 had installed the simplification, Chapters 6–8 stage the rewriting — the commandments altered one by one to license every transgression of the principles, the propaganda apparatus now no longer simplifying the principles but actively converting them into their opposites while the wall on which they are painted appears unchanged.

• Technique + evidence: Orwell deploys the rewriting of the commandments paired with the animals' silent bewilderment — the commandment "No animal shall sleep in a bed" (Chapter 2) becomes "No animal shall sleep in a bed with sheets" (Chapter 6); "No animal shall drink alcohol" becomes "No animal shall drink alcohol to excess" (Chapter 8); "No animal shall kill any other animal" becomes "No animal shall kill any other animal without cause" (Chapter 8) — combining the formal device of the rewritten commandment (each addition consisting of two or three words that license the transgression while preserving the appearance of the original principle) with the animals' silent bewilderment (Clover's recurring "surely it had been different in the old days", the gap between memory and text the technique is engineered to widen).

• Close analysis: The qualifier "with sheets" performs the rewriting precisely because it consists of two words — the smallest possible alteration that nevertheless permits the entire transgression — while the prepositional phrases "to excess" and "without cause" perform the same operation, the close-analysis grammar exposing that the propaganda requires only the smallest grammatical addition to convert any principle into its opposite.

• Effect on reader 1: Thus the reader sees the rewriting in real time — the commandments altered between chapters in the reader's view, the animals' inability to register the change generating the same horror the simplification began to install.

• Effect on reader 2: Indeed, the reader recognises that the propaganda apparatus is not merely defending the regime against criticism but actively converting the principles by which the regime could be criticised into the principles by which the regime can defend itself — the language of accountability captured by the language of the regime.

• Author's purpose: Orwell may be demonstrating that the rewriting of the commandments is the central instrument of the regime — more central than the dogs, more central than the executions, more central than the appropriation — because the rewriting eliminates the standard against which the regime could be measured, and a regime that has captured the standard cannot be held to it.

• Context (AO3): Stalinist photographs of the early Bolsheviks were retouched across the 1930s to remove Trotsky, Kamenev and Zinoviev from the historical record; the Short Course on the History of the All-Union Communist Party (Bolsheviks) (1938) rewrote the entire revolutionary history to align with Stalin's preferred narrative — the rewriting of the commandments in Chapters 6–8 condenses the textual operation Stalinism perfected into the wall of the big barn.

BODY PARAGRAPH 3 — Chapters 8–10: cult and replacement anthem (7 sentences — about 230 words)

Scene focus: "Beasts of England" abolished in Chapter 7 and replaced by Minimus's "Comrade Napoleon" in Chapter 8; Napoleon's accumulating titles; the final commandment.

• Topic sentence: Where Chapters 6–8 had rewritten the commandments, Chapters 8–10 stage the language's final consolidation — the original revolutionary anthem abolished and replaced by the personality cult, Napoleon's titles inflating beyond rational evaluation, and the final commandment "All animals are equal but some animals are more equal than others" completing the regime's linguistic capture of the revolution's founding principle.

• Technique + evidence: Orwell deploys oxymoron inside cumulative rewriting — the final commandment in Chapter 10 — "ALL ANIMALS ARE EQUAL BUT SOME ANIMALS ARE MORE EQUAL THAN OTHERS" — combining oxymoron (the contradictory yoking of "equal" and "more equal", the comparative rendering the absolute meaningless within the same sentence) with cumulative rewriting (the entire seven commandments collapsed into a single line, the conclusion of the operation that began in Chapter 3 with the simplification and continued in Chapters 6–8 with the additions).

• Close analysis: The capitalised typography in the original text registers the regime's appropriation of public space — the commandment now the only text on the wall — while the comparative "more equal" exposes the propaganda's most audacious move: the conversion of the founding principle of equality into the licence for the inequality the regime has produced, the syntax enacting the inversion it announces.

• Effect on reader 1: Thus the reader feels the closure of the linguistic operation — the rewriting and the simplification and the cult having consumed every prior register, the wall now displaying the single line that licenses everything the regime has done.

• Effect on reader 2: Indeed, the reader recognises that the language of the revolution has been completely captured — that there is no longer a vocabulary in which the regime could be criticised, because the regime has appropriated even the words by which the criticism could be articulated.

• Author's purpose: Orwell may be arguing that the totalitarian regime's most lethal achievement is not the coup but the linguistic capture — that the regime which holds the language holds the future, and that the citizens whose language has been captured cannot resist a regime they cannot name.

• Context (AO3): Stalin's official titles by the late 1930s included "Brilliant Genius of Humanity", "Father of Nations", "Gardener of Human Happiness" — the cataloguing satire of Napoleon's titles in Chapter 8 is the historical pattern condensed into one paragraph, and the slogan "All animals are equal but some animals are more equal than others" condenses the entire Stalinist linguistic operation into a single sentence.

CONCLUSION (4 sentences — about 150 words)

• Restated thesis: In conclusion, Orwell dramatises propaganda and the manipulation of language as the totalitarian regime's most economical instrument — Chapter 3 installing the simplification, Chapters 6–8 escalating into the rewriting, Chapters 8–10 consummating in the cult and the final oxymoron.

• Controlling concept: What Chapter 3 installed as the simplification of complex truth, Chapters 6–8 escalated as the rewriting of the principles, Chapters 8–10 completed as the linguistic capture of the revolution's vocabulary — three stages of the same operation, the language the first thing the new tyranny corrupts to disguise what it is doing.

• Author's purpose: Writing in the same year as "Politics and the English Language" (April 1946), Orwell uses the novella to dramatise, at the level of fictional incident, the linguistic operation the essay would diagnose at the level of analysis — the citizens' defence of clear thought as the prophylactic against the regime that requires vague abstract prose.

• Ultimate moral: The language is the future the regime requires — and the reader who closes the novella with the rewritten commandments lodged in memory has been trained to recognise the operation when it appears in the next regime's vocabulary, which is the analytical move the essay names as the citizen's defence.

Q8 — Class, Inequality and Hierarchy

Question

How does Orwell explore ideas about class and inequality in Animal Farm?

Write about:

- what different characters say and do - how Orwell presents ideas about class and inequality.

[30 marks + 4 AO4 = 34 marks]

Frame

Class is the disease the revolution was meant to cure and the disease the revolution reproduces — the human hierarchy under Jones, the egalitarian principles installed in Chapter 2, and the renamed Manor Farm and the closing dinner in Chapter 10. The plan tracks three stages — Jones's old hierarchy, the egalitarian moment, the new hierarchy.

Technique gloss:

• Cyclical structure — Manor Farm renamed Animal Farm renamed Manor Farm; the form is the inequality.

• Allegorical hierarchy — pigs (Bolshevik leadership) / dogs (NKVD) / sheep (propagandised masses) / horses (working class) / Mr Pilkington (Western capitalist powers) / Mr Frederick (Nazi Germany).

• Foil pairing — the Seven Commandments (Chapter 2) against the single rewritten commandment (Chapter 10).

• Symbolic geography — the farmhouse (the master's house) against the pasture (the working space of the animal majority).

• Choric repetition — the sheep's bleating drowning out the few moments dissent might have been heard.

• Visual climax — the pigs and humans dining together at the closing dinner.

INTRODUCTION (3 sentences — about 120 words)

• Hook (historical fact): In 1917, the wealthiest one per cent of Russian households controlled an estimated quarter of the empire's wealth while approximately seventy-eight per cent of the population were peasants whose conditions had changed little since the abolition of serfdom in 1861 — the inequality that the October Revolution claimed to dismantle and that, by the time Animal Farm was published in August 1945, the Soviet nomenklatura system had reconstructed under new vocabulary.

• Building sentence (historical context): Orwell, who described himself as a "democratic socialist" in "Why I Write" (1946), was therefore not writing a critique of the principle of equality but a warning that the principle would be reproduced as inequality by any leadership that did not specify, in advance, the accountability mechanisms by which the new hierarchy would be prevented.

• Thesis: Across Jones's pre-revolutionary hierarchy in which the human master controls the animal labour, the Chapter 2 egalitarian moment in which the Seven Commandments install the principle of "All animals are equal", and the Chapter 10 closing dinner in which the renamed Manor Farm and the indistinguishability of pig and man announce the new hierarchy, Orwell presents class and inequality as the disease the revolution must, in advance, specify the prophylactic against — failing which the new hierarchy will reproduce the old in vocabulary the masses can no longer recognise as different.

BODY PARAGRAPH 1 — Chapter 1: Jones's hierarchy (7 sentences — about 220 words)

Scene focus: Jones's drunken neglect; Old Major's address against the human master.

• Topic sentence: Orwell installs the pre-revolutionary hierarchy not as the diffuse social condition but as the specific operation of one master's control over the animal majority — Jones's neglect, his drunkenness, his failure to feed the animals at the opening of Chapter 1 the specific case the revolution is engineered to overthrow.

• Technique + evidence: Orwell deploys lucid prose paired with the catalogue of human exploitation — the opening sentence "Mr. Jones, of the Manor Farm, had locked the hen-houses for the night, but was too drunk to remember to shut the popholes" (Chapter 1) — combining lucid prose (the windowpane register, the short Anglo-Saxon vocabulary, the absence of qualification or metaphor) with bathetic specificity (the proper noun "Mr. Jones, of the Manor Farm" registering the master in the bureaucratic register of land ownership, the closing detail "too drunk to remember to shut the popholes" exposing the negligence the revolution will subsequently overthrow) inside the same opening line.

• Close analysis: The conjunction "but" in "but was too drunk" registers the structural contradiction the revolution will name — the master who has the responsibility he has not earned and is incompetent to fulfil — while the proper noun "Mr. Jones, of the Manor Farm" establishes the bureaucratic register that makes the inequality invisible to those who benefit from it.

• Effect on reader 1: Thus the reader hears the lucid prose of the opening register the hierarchy as the structural condition rather than personal vice — the inequality the consequence of Jones's office, not Jones's character.

• Effect on reader 2: Indeed, the reader registers the controlling concept's foundation — the inequality the revolution will overthrow is genuine, the hierarchy that produces Jones is structural, and the case for revolution is therefore honourable.

• Author's purpose: Orwell may be arguing that the pre-revolutionary hierarchy is not a misunderstanding to be cleared up but a structural condition to be dismantled — and that the revolution's case is therefore not refutable by pointing to the betrayal that follows it.

• Context (AO3): Tsar Nicholas II ruled the Russian Empire from 1894 until the February Revolution of 1917; his administration's incompetence in feeding the urban workforce during the First World War, its repressive response to the 1905 Revolution, and the autocratic structure of the Romanov state form the historical pattern Orwell condenses into Mr Jones's drunken neglect of the popholes.

BODY PARAGRAPH 2 — Chapter 2: the egalitarian moment (7 sentences — about 230 words)

Scene focus: the Seven Commandments are painted on the barn wall; "All animals are equal" is the seventh and last.

• Topic sentence: Where Chapter 1 had installed Jones's hierarchy, Chapter 2 stages the egalitarian moment — the Seven Commandments painted on the barn wall, the principle "All animals are equal" installed as the seventh and last, and the entire animal community committing to the principle in the form of public, durable text.

• Technique + evidence: Orwell deploys the codification of the Seven Commandments paired with the visual permanence of the barn wall — "They had been painted on the tarred wall in great white letters that could be read thirty yards away" (Chapter 2) — combining the codification of the principles (the seven commandments converting Animalism from oral vision into public text, the codification the institutional move the revolution requires) with the visual permanence of the barn wall (the "great white letters" and the spatial measure "thirty yards away" registering the principles as visible to every animal at every moment, the public visibility the procedural form of accountability the principles require).

• Close analysis: The phrase "that could be read thirty yards away" registers the principles' precondition — that the animals can read them — and exposes, retrospectively, the gap the regime will exploit: the sheep cannot read; many of the other animals can read only the first letters; the literacy gap is the precondition of the rewriting that will follow.

• Effect on reader 1: Thus the reader feels the institutional achievement of the egalitarian moment — the principles installed in public text, visible to every animal, the procedural form of accountability that the gap Old Major's vision left required.

• Effect on reader 2: Indeed, the reader registers the structural fragility — the principles installed at the level of text but not at the level of literacy, the public text only as durable as the animals' capacity to read it, and the literacy gap therefore the gap through which the rewriting will subsequently operate.

• Author's purpose: Orwell may be arguing that the principle of equality requires not only its public articulation but the cognitive equipment by which the masses can read it — and that any revolution that codifies the principles without distributing the literacy is the revolution whose principles can be rewritten without the masses noticing.

• Context (AO3): Soviet literacy campaigns from the 1920s onward did dramatically increase basic literacy, but the Short Course on the History of the All-Union Communist Party (Bolsheviks) (1938) effectively monopolised the historical record in a single text taught across the entire educational system — the literacy that the revolution distributed was, by the late 1930s, literacy in the regime's preferred narrative rather than the principles the revolution had installed.

BODY PARAGRAPH 3 — Chapter 10: the new hierarchy (7 sentences — about 235 words)

Scene focus: the closing dinner with Pilkington; the farm is renamed Manor Farm; the final commandment.

• Topic sentence: Where Chapter 2 had installed the egalitarian principles, Chapter 10 stages the new hierarchy — the renamed Manor Farm, the final commandment that licenses inequality, the closing dinner that allies the pigs with the very humans the revolution was launched to overthrow — the structural form of the controlling concept's claim that unaccountable revolution reproduces the hierarchy it overthrew.

• Technique + evidence: Orwell deploys symbolic renaming inside cyclical closure — "the name 'Animal Farm' had been abolished. Henceforward the farm was to be known as 'The Manor Farm' — which, he believed, was its correct and original name" (Chapter 10, Napoleon speaking to Pilkington) — combining symbolic renaming (the act of public re-designation the formal completion of the cycle the novella has been tracing across ten chapters) with cyclical closure (the "correct and original" registering the regime's claim that the new hierarchy is not a betrayal of the revolution but the restoration of the natural order, the closure of the cycle by way of its rhetorical denial) inside the same announcement.

• Close analysis: The adjectives "correct and original" perform the regime's most audacious linguistic move — the pre-revolutionary hierarchy reframed as the natural state, the revolution itself rewritten as deviation — while the pluperfect "had been abolished" erases the agency of the abolition, the syntax enacting the disappearance of the egalitarian moment from the historical record.

• Effect on reader 1: Thus the reader feels the closure of the cycle as the structural form of the controlling concept — the hierarchy reproduced not despite the revolution but as its consequence, the renaming the linguistic announcement that the regime has captured the vocabulary by which the revolution could be defended.

• Effect on reader 2: Indeed, the reader recognises the new hierarchy as more lethal than the old — the pre-revolutionary hierarchy was vulnerable to the revolution that overthrew it, while the new hierarchy is invulnerable to the language by which it could be criticised.

• Author's purpose: Orwell may be demonstrating that the controlling concept's most disturbing implication is structural — that the revolution which overthrew the hierarchy is the same revolution that reproduces it, that the only prophylactic is the accountability mechanism Old Major's vision did not specify, and that the citizens of any post-revolutionary regime must therefore demand that mechanism in the language of the principle the regime has captured.

• Context (AO3): Stalin adopted Tsarist trappings — military uniforms, palatial residences, hierarchical titles — across the late 1930s and 1940s; the November 1943 Tehran Conference saw him received as co-architect of the post-war order alongside Churchill and Roosevelt; the closing dinner of Animal Farm condenses that historical recognition into the public text of the renamed Manor Farm.

CONCLUSION (4 sentences — about 150 words)

• Restated thesis: In conclusion, Orwell anatomises class and inequality as the disease the revolution must, in advance, specify the prophylactic against — Jones's hierarchy in Chapter 1, the egalitarian moment in Chapter 2, the new hierarchy in Chapter 10.

• Controlling concept: What Chapter 1 installed as Jones's structural negligence, Chapter 2 codified as the public principles, Chapter 10 reproduced as the renamed Manor Farm — three stages of the same hierarchy, the consistency confirming the controlling concept's claim that unaccountable revolution reproduces the inequality it overthrew.

• Author's purpose: Writing as a self-described democratic socialist for a 1945 readership that Labour would, in July 1945, elect on a platform of welfare and nationalisation, Orwell uses the inequality arc to insist that the principle of equality is honourable and that the institutional design must be specific enough to defend the principle against the leadership the principle produces.

• Ultimate moral: The renaming of the farm is the regime's announcement that the hierarchy has reproduced — and the reader who closes the novella with the closing image lodged in memory has been trained to recognise the operation when it is repeated under new vocabulary, the analytical move that protects every subsequent revolution from the new Manor Farm.

Q9 — Fear and Violence

Question

How does Orwell explore ideas about fear and violence in Animal Farm?

Write about:

- what different characters do - how Orwell presents ideas about fear and violence.

[30 marks + 4 AO4 = 34 marks]

Frame

Fear and violence are the regime's permanent foundation — installed through the secret training of the dogs in Chapter 3, escalated through the public executions in Chapter 7, and consummated through the institutional violence of the closing chapters. The plan tracks three stages — covert preparation, public spectacle, institutional invisibility.

Technique gloss:

• Allegorical mapping — the dogs = the NKVD, the Soviet secret police; the public executions = the Moscow show trials and the Great Terror.

• Auditory escalation — the dogs' "baying" arriving before the bodies; the screams of the executed.

• Ekphrastic violence — Orwell's plain prose registering the throat-tearing without protective metaphor.

• Symbolic preparation — Napoleon takes the puppies "to make himself responsible for their education" (Chapter 3).

• Choric inversion — the sheep's bleating drowning out the dissent the violence is engineered to suppress.

• Spatial public spectacle — the killing knoll, the public execution site of Chapter 7.

INTRODUCTION (3 sentences — about 120 words)

• Hook (historical fact): The Soviet NKVD secret police, founded in 1934 from the predecessor OGPU, conducted the Great Terror of 1936–38 that imprisoned approximately 1.5 million people, executed approximately 700,000 of them, and ran the Moscow show trials at which Zinoviev and Kamenev (1936) and Bukharin (1938) confessed under torture to fabricated charges of treason — the historical pattern Orwell condenses into the dogs' throat-tearing of the confessing animals on the floor of the big barn.

• Building sentence (historical context): Orwell, who had served in the POUM militia in Spain in 1936–37 and watched Stalinist forces suppress the POUM in Barcelona's May Days of 1937, had direct biographical experience of the operations the novella would subsequently dramatise — the Homage to Catalonia (1938) record of those weeks providing the testimonial spine that Animal Farm converts into beast fable.

• Thesis: Across the Chapter 3 covert preparation in which Napoleon takes the puppies and trains them in private, the Chapter 7 public spectacle in which the dogs tear the throats of the confessing animals on the killing knoll, and the Chapters 8–10 institutional invisibility in which the violence becomes ambient — whips carried openly, fear so internalised that coercion is no longer visibly necessary — Orwell presents fear and violence as the regime's permanent foundation, the institutional spine without which every other operation would collapse.

BODY PARAGRAPH 1 — Chapter 3: covert preparation (7 sentences — about 220 words)

Scene focus: Napoleon takes the puppies away from their mothers "to make himself responsible for their education".

• Topic sentence: Orwell installs the regime's violence not as the regime's first move but as its precondition — the secret training of the dogs in Chapter 3 occurring while the rest of the farm believes the revolution has already been won, the violence prepared in private long before its public deployment.

• Technique + evidence: Orwell deploys euphemism inside spatial isolation — "Napoleon took them away from their mothers, saying that he would make himself responsible for their education, and put them in a loft so secluded that the rest of the farm soon forgot their existence" (Chapter 3) — combining euphemism (the noun "education" converting militarisation into pedagogy, the regime's preferred linguistic register pre-applied to the operation it conceals) with spatial isolation (the loft "so secluded" removing the violence from public view, the geography itself the precondition of the deployment to come) inside the same passage.

• Close analysis: The pluperfect "had taken them away" and the closing "soon forgot their existence" register the collective forgetting that the regime's violence requires — the violence prepared in the same chapter as the milk and apples appropriation, the corruption and the violence both seeded in the revolution's first weeks.

• Effect on reader 1: Thus the reader feels the dramatic irony of the scene — the puppies' future use as terror-instruments visible to the reader before the animals can recognise the preparation — generating mounting dread the rest of the novella will subsequently confirm.

• Effect on reader 2: Indeed, the reader registers the structural method of totalitarian violence — that the violence is not improvisational but premeditated, the instruments of state terror assembled in secret long before they are deployed.

• Author's purpose: Orwell may be arguing that the totalitarian regime's violence is the regime's foundation rather than its accessory — that no regime sustains itself on rhetoric alone, that the dogs are required from the first chapter onward, and that the citizens of any post-revolutionary regime must demand transparency over the security apparatus the new leadership cultivates.

• Context (AO3): The OGPU, founded in 1923 as the successor to the Cheka and reorganised as the NKVD in 1934, was cultivated under Stalin's personal supervision across the 1920s — the institution Stalin used in 1936–38 for the Great Terror was the institution he had built in private during the years his rivals focused on policy debates.

BODY PARAGRAPH 2 — Chapter 7: public spectacle (7 sentences — about 235 words)

Scene focus: the public executions in the big barn; the four young pigs and the hens confess and are torn apart by the dogs.

• Topic sentence: Where Chapter 3 had installed the violence as covert preparation, Chapter 7 stages it as public spectacle — the executions in the big barn the regime's pedagogy, the killed animals not merely victims but lessons, teaching every survivor that the cost of dissent is destruction.

• Technique + evidence: Orwell deploys ekphrastic plain prose paired with the regime's terrible voice — "When they had finished their confession, the dogs promptly tore their throats out, and in a terrible voice Napoleon demanded whether any other animal had anything to confess" (Chapter 7) — combining ekphrastic plain prose (the verb "tore" registering the violence in lucid Anglo-Saxon vocabulary, the adverb "promptly" converting the killing into procedural efficiency) with the regime's terrible voice (Napoleon's "terrible voice" converting the spectacle into pedagogy, the demand for further confession transforming the survivors into the regime's permanent audience) inside the same sentence.

• Close analysis: The conjunction "and" linking confession to execution registers the impossibility of mercy within the sentence structure itself — the syntax enacting the procedural efficiency it is naming — while the verb "demanded" converts voluntary confession into compelled performance, the regime's pedagogy operating inside the grammar that records it.

• Effect on reader 1: Thus the reader experiences the executions as the regime's pedagogical climax — the violence not random but designed, every survivor instructed, the killing knoll the geography of the lesson.

• Effect on reader 2: Indeed, the reader recognises the structural function of the spectacle — that the public execution is not merely cruelty but communication, the killed animals the medium through which the regime addresses every survivor across the rest of the chapters.

• Author's purpose: Orwell may be demonstrating that the totalitarian regime's violence operates as pedagogy rather than as punishment — that the executions are designed not to remove the executed but to instruct the survivors, and that the survivors' silence across the remaining chapters is therefore the lesson successfully learnt.

• Context (AO3): The Moscow show trials of 1936–38 — Zinoviev and Kamenev (August 1936), Pyatakov and Radek (January 1937), Bukharin and Yagoda (March 1938) — saw the original Bolshevik leadership confess under torture to fabricated charges of treason and be executed by the NKVD; the public spectacle was designed to terrorise the entire population, and Chapter 7's executions condense the operation into a single afternoon on the killing knoll.

BODY PARAGRAPH 3 — Chapters 8–10: institutional invisibility (7 sentences — about 230 words)

Scene focus: the whip in Napoleon's trotter (Chapter 10); the dogs gambolling around him; the institutional violence so internalised that coercion is no longer visibly necessary.

• Topic sentence: Where Chapter 7 had staged the violence as public spectacle, Chapters 8–10 consummate it as institutional invisibility — the violence no longer requiring spectacle because the lesson has been learnt, the whip in Napoleon's trotter and the dogs gambolling beside him the visual condensation of a regime that has internalised its violence into the routine.

• Technique + evidence: Orwell deploys symbolic action inside reversed verb register — "Out came Napoleon himself, majestically upright, casting haughty glances from side to side, and with his dogs gambolling round him. He carried a whip in his trotter" (Chapter 10) — combining symbolic action (the whip in the trotter condensing the entire allegory's argument into a single visual image, the revolutionary now physically the master) with reversed verb register (the verb "gambolling" applied to the dogs that had previously "dashed" and "baying", the apparatus of terror domesticated into pageantry) inside the same passage.

• Close analysis: The adverb "majestically" and the adjective "haughty" register the transfer of human aristocratic vocabulary onto the boar — the linguistic completion of the visual transformation — while the dogs' "gambolling" registers the institutional violence as the regime's permanent pet, the threat no longer requiring the verbs of attack because the lesson of Chapter 7 has rendered the threat structural.

• Effect on reader 1: Thus the reader feels the violence's institutional consummation — the whip carried openly and the dogs domesticated into courtiers the visual form of a regime whose violence has become so embedded that it no longer requires the spectacles of Chapter 7.

• Effect on reader 2: Indeed, the reader recognises the controlling concept's most lethal implication — that the regime's success is measured by the invisibility of its violence, and that the citizens whose silence the regime has secured are the citizens who can no longer name the violence as violence.

• Author's purpose: Orwell may be arguing that the regime's violence is most lethal when it has become institutional — that the whip in the trotter is the regime's announcement that the spectacle is no longer required, and that the citizens' apparent calm is therefore the structural form of the regime's victory rather than evidence of its absence.

• Context (AO3): By the late 1940s the Soviet Union had embedded the NKVD's successor (the MGB, then the KGB after 1954) into the structure of the state to the point that the spectacular show trials of the 1930s had been replaced by the routine bureaucratic terror Solzhenitsyn would document in The Gulag Archipelago (1973) — the institutional invisibility the closing chapters of Animal Farm anticipate.

CONCLUSION (4 sentences — about 150 words)

• Restated thesis: In conclusion, Orwell stages fear and violence as the regime's permanent foundation — Chapter 3 covert preparation, Chapter 7 public spectacle, Chapters 8–10 institutional invisibility.

• Controlling concept: What Chapter 3 installed as the puppies' secret education, Chapter 7 escalated as the throat-tearing on the killing knoll, Chapters 8–10 consummated as the whip in the trotter — three stages of the same operation, the violence the institutional spine without which the rewriting and the appropriation would collapse.

• Author's purpose: Writing from his own Spanish experience of watching Stalinist forces suppress the POUM in Barcelona in 1937, Orwell uses the violence arc to demonstrate that the totalitarian regime's foundation is the security apparatus the leadership cultivates in private, and that the citizens of any post-revolutionary regime must demand transparency over that apparatus before it acquires the institutional invisibility that renders it unaccountable.

• Ultimate moral: The whip in the trotter is the regime's announcement that the lesson has been learnt — and the reader who recognises the visual condensation has been trained to demand of every leadership the transparency over the security apparatus that the citizens of Animal Farm did not.

Q10 — The Seven Commandments and the Rewriting of History

Question

How does Orwell use the Seven Commandments to explore ideas about revolution and its betrayal in Animal Farm?

Write about:

- how the Seven Commandments change across the novella - how Orwell uses the Seven Commandments to convey his ideas about revolution and its betrayal.

[30 marks + 4 AO4 = 34 marks]

Frame

The Seven Commandments are the moral spine of the regime, and their gradual rewriting is the novella's central structural argument — the codification of the principles in Chapter 2, the rewriting one by one across Chapters 6–8, the collapse into the single oxymoron of Chapter 10. The plan tracks three stages — codification, rewriting, collapse.

Technique gloss:

• Codification — the conversion of oral vision into public text; the seven commandments painted on the barn wall.

• Pluperfect rewriting — "had seemed", "had been"; the grammatical instrument of historical falsification.

• Smallest grammatical alteration — the addition of "with sheets", "to excess", "without cause"; two- or three-word additions licensing the entire transgression.

• Cumulative rewriting — the seven commandments collapsed into a single line by Chapter 10.

• Oxymoron — "All animals are equal but some animals are more equal than others"; the founding principle and its inversion in the same sentence.

• Allegorical mapping — the rewriting of the commandments = the Stalinist rewriting of the historical record.

INTRODUCTION (3 sentences — about 120 words)

• Hook (historical fact): The Short Course on the History of the All-Union Communist Party (Bolsheviks) was published in 1938 under Stalin's editorial supervision and rewrote the entire history of the Russian Revolution to align with the regime's preferred narrative — Trotsky erased, Stalin's role inflated, the Old Bolsheviks executed in the show trials retroactively converted into traitors — and the textbook was taught as the official history across every educational level of the Soviet Union for the next two decades.

• Building sentence (historical context): Orwell, who had watched Stalinist photographs of the early Bolsheviks be retouched across the 1930s to remove Trotsky, Kamenev and Zinoviev from the historical record (the practice meticulously documented by David King in The Commissar Vanishes, 1997), constructs the rewriting of the Seven Commandments as the textual form of the visual erasure — the rewriting of the past the regime's most lethal instrument because it removes the standard against which the regime could be measured.

• Thesis: Across the Chapter 2 codification of the Seven Commandments in "great white letters" on the barn wall, the Chapters 6–8 rewriting one by one to license every transgression, and the Chapter 10 collapse into the single oxymoron "All animals are equal but some animals are more equal than others", Orwell uses the Seven Commandments as the novella's central structural argument — the rewriting of the principles the most economical instrument of the regime, the language of accountability captured by the language of the regime.

BODY PARAGRAPH 1 — Chapter 2: codification (7 sentences — about 220 words)

Scene focus: the Seven Commandments are painted on the barn wall in "great white letters" immediately after the revolution.

• Topic sentence: Orwell installs the Seven Commandments not as the regime's first move but as the revolution's institutional achievement — the conversion of Old Major's oral vision into public, durable text the procedural form of accountability the principles require, the codification the structural complement to the diagnosis Chapter 1 articulated.

• Technique + evidence: Orwell deploys the codification of the principles paired with the visual permanence of the public text — the seven commandments are "painted on the tarred wall in great white letters that could be read thirty yards away" and "there, in unalterable form, were the rules by which the animals of Animal Farm must live for ever after" (Chapter 2) — combining the codification of the principles (the conversion of the oral vision into public text, the institutional move the revolution requires) with the visual permanence of the public text (the "great white letters" and the spatial measure "thirty yards away" registering the principles as visible to every animal at every moment) inside the same passage.

• Close analysis: The phrase "in unalterable form" registers the principles' precondition — the assumption that the form is unalterable — while the temporal "for ever after" exposes, retrospectively, the gap the regime will exploit: the assumption of permanence is precisely what permits the rewriting that follows, the form unalterable only as long as no one is permitted to alter it.

• Effect on reader 1: Thus the reader feels the institutional achievement of the codification — the principles installed in public text, visible to every animal, the procedural form of accountability that the gap Old Major's vision left required.

• Effect on reader 2: Indeed, the reader registers the structural fragility — the principles installed at the level of text but not at the level of literacy, the public text only as durable as the masses' capacity to read it, and the assumption of unalterability therefore the assumption the regime will subsequently exploit.

• Author's purpose: Orwell may be arguing that the institutional codification of the principles is the necessary but insufficient condition of accountability — that the public text requires not only its installation but the literacy by which the masses can defend it, and that any revolution that codifies without distributing the literacy is the revolution whose principles can be rewritten without the masses noticing.

• Context (AO3): The 1918 Constitution of the Russian Soviet Federative Socialist Republic codified universal suffrage, the abolition of class distinctions, and the principle of "All Power to the Soviets" — yet by the early 1920s the soviets had been effectively replaced by Communist Party committees, and by 1936 the Stalin Constitution preserved the rhetoric while installing the centralised personal authority of the General Secretary; the gap between the codified principles and the institutional reality is the historical pattern Orwell condenses into the barn wall.

BODY PARAGRAPH 2 — Chapters 6–8: rewriting (7 sentences — about 235 words)

Scene focus: the commandment "No animal shall sleep in a bed" becomes "No animal shall sleep in a bed with sheets" in Chapter 6; "No animal shall drink alcohol" becomes "No animal shall drink alcohol to excess" in Chapter 8; "No animal shall kill any other animal" becomes "No animal shall kill any other animal without cause" in Chapter 8.

• Topic sentence: Where Chapter 2 had installed the codification, Chapters 6–8 stage the rewriting — the commandments altered one by one to license every transgression of the principles, the wall on which they are painted appearing unchanged while the principles painted on it are progressively converted into their opposites.

• Technique + evidence: Orwell deploys the smallest grammatical alteration paired with the animals' silent bewilderment — Clover's response to the rewritten commandment about beds, "surely there had been no clause in the Fourth Commandment mentioning sheets" (Chapter 6, paraphrased) — combining the smallest grammatical alteration (the addition of "with sheets" consisting of two words, the smallest possible alteration that nevertheless permits the entire transgression) with the animals' silent bewilderment (the gap between memory and text, Clover's near-recognition the structural form of the working class's tragedy) inside the same scene.

• Close analysis: The qualifier "with sheets" performs the rewriting precisely because it consists of two words — the smallest possible alteration — while the modal "surely" in Clover's response registers the technique's efficacy: the doubt arrives as a softened concession rather than a confident accusation, the syntax of the doubt itself the regime's evidence that the rewriting has worked.

• Effect on reader 1: Thus the reader sees the rewriting in real time — the commandments altered between chapters in the reader's view, Clover's softened "surely" registering the cognitive distance that the rewriting has produced.

• Effect on reader 2: Indeed, the reader recognises that the rewriting is engineered to operate inside the gap between memory and confidence — that the working class remembers but cannot confidently assert, and that the regime requires the softened "surely" rather than the confident accusation, the regime's victory the production of doubt rather than the elimination of memory.

• Author's purpose: Orwell may be demonstrating that the regime's most lethal instrument is the rewriting of the standard rather than the breach of the standard — that a regime which has captured the standard cannot be held to it, and that the citizens' defence of the principles therefore requires the literacy and the confidence to assert the original text against the rewritten one.

• Context (AO3): Stalinist photographs of the early Bolsheviks were retouched across the 1930s to remove Trotsky, Kamenev and Zinoviev from the historical record — David King's The Commissar Vanishes (1997) documents the pattern in detail — and the rewriting of the commandments in Chapters 6–8 is the textual form of that visual erasure.

BODY PARAGRAPH 3 — Chapter 10: collapse (7 sentences — about 235 words)

Scene focus: the seven commandments collapse into the single line "All animals are equal but some animals are more equal than others".

• Topic sentence: Where Chapters 6–8 had rewritten the commandments one by one, Chapter 10 stages the collapse — the seven commandments reduced to a single line, the principle of equality and its inversion held inside the same sentence, the wall now displaying the regime's most audacious linguistic move: the conversion of the founding principle into the licence for everything the principle was installed to prevent.

• Technique + evidence: Orwell deploys oxymoron paired with capitalised public text — "There was nothing there now except a single Commandment. It ran: ALL ANIMALS ARE EQUAL BUT SOME ANIMALS ARE MORE EQUAL THAN OTHERS" (Chapter 10) — combining oxymoron (the contradictory yoking of "equal" and "more equal", the comparative rendering the absolute meaningless within the same sentence) with capitalised public text (the typography registering the regime's appropriation of the public space, the commandment now the only text on the wall) inside the same revelation.

• Close analysis: The comparative "more equal" exposes the regime's most audacious move precisely because the comparative cannot grammatically modify an absolute — "equal" admits no degrees — and the impossibility of the formulation registers, at the level of grammar, the impossibility of the regime the formulation licenses.

• Effect on reader 1: Thus the reader feels the collapse of the codification as the structural form of the controlling concept's claim — the principles consumed by the regime that was installed to defend them, the wall now the regime's billboard rather than the revolution's constitution.

• Effect on reader 2: Indeed, the reader recognises that the collapse is the rewriting's terminal move — that the regime no longer requires the smallest grammatical alteration because the seven commandments themselves have been replaced, and that the citizens of the regime no longer have a standard against which to measure it.

• Author's purpose: Orwell may be arguing that the regime's most lethal achievement is the linguistic capture of the principles — that the regime which has rewritten the standard is the regime that the citizens of the subsequent generation cannot resist because they cannot name it as different from what it replaced, and that the citizens' defence of clear thought is therefore the prophylactic against the regime that requires vague abstract prose.

• Context (AO3): The Short Course on the History of the All-Union Communist Party (Bolsheviks) (1938) effectively monopolised the historical record in a single text taught across the entire educational system of the Soviet Union; the original revolutionary documents were progressively withdrawn from circulation; the citizens of the regime were left with the regime's preferred narrative as the only text against which the regime could be measured — the structural pattern Orwell condenses into the collapse of the seven commandments into a single line.

CONCLUSION (4 sentences — about 150 words)

• Restated thesis: In conclusion, Orwell uses the Seven Commandments as the novella's central structural argument — Chapter 2 codifying the principles, Chapters 6–8 rewriting them one by one, Chapter 10 collapsing them into the single oxymoron.

• Controlling concept: What Chapter 2 installed as the institutional achievement of public text, Chapters 6–8 escalated as the rewriting of the standard, Chapter 10 consummated as the collapse into the founding-principle inversion — three stages of the same operation, the language of accountability captured by the language of the regime.

• Author's purpose: Writing the same year as "Politics and the English Language" (April 1946), Orwell uses the rewriting of the commandments to demonstrate, at the level of fictional incident, the linguistic operation the essay would diagnose at the level of analysis — the citizens' defence of clear thought as the prophylactic against the regime that requires vague abstract prose.

• Ultimate moral: The collapse of the seven commandments into the single oxymoron is the regime's announcement that the standard has been captured — and the reader who closes the novella with the rewritten commandment lodged in memory has been trained to demand of every regime the original text against which the regime can be measured, the analytical move that protects every subsequent revolution from the new Manor Farm.

Three context factors that travel across all 10 themes

Every essay should thread one of these into one BP. Pick the factor whose period-fit is sharpest for the Q.

1. The Russian Revolution 1917 — Lenin's return + Bolshevik takeover + Civil War 1918–22. Best fit: Q4 Old Major, Q6 Power and Corruption, Q8 Class and Inequality, Q10 Seven Commandments. Why it lands: Old Major condenses Marx and Lenin; the revolution's egalitarian principles and the Civil War's defence of those principles are the historical foundation against which the subsequent betrayal is measured.

2. Stalin vs Trotsky 1924–29 + the Great Terror 1936–38 + the NKVD + the Moscow show trials. Best fit: Q1 Napoleon, Q2 Snowball, Q5 Squealer, Q6 Power and Corruption, Q9 Fear and Violence. Why it lands: Trotsky's expulsion and exile (1927–29) are the historical anchor for Snowball; the Great Terror is the historical anchor for the public executions; the NKVD is the historical anchor for the dogs; the Moscow show trials are the historical anchor for the rewriting of the commandments.

3. Orwell's Spain 1936–37 + suppression of POUM by Stalinist forces in Barcelona May 1937 + 1939 Molotov-Ribbentrop Pact + 1943 Tehran Conference + 1945 publication after Faber, Gollancz and Cape rejection. Best fit: Q1 Napoleon, Q2 Snowball, Q6 Power and Corruption, Q7 Propaganda and Language, Q8 Class and Inequality. Why it lands: Orwell's biographical experience of Stalinist forces suppressing the POUM is the direct source of the betrayal-of-revolution thesis; the Molotov-Ribbentrop Pact (August 1939) is the historical anchor for Napoleon's deal with Frederick; the Tehran Conference (November 1943) is the historical anchor for the closing dinner.

---

Two critical voices that travel across all 10 themes

Every essay should name one critic, with year, in one BP or in the conclusion. Pick the voice whose claim is sharpest for the Q.

1. George Orwell, "Why I Write" (1946) and "Politics and the English Language" (April 1946). Orwell describes himself in "Why I Write" as a "democratic socialist" and identifies Animal Farm as "the first book in which I tried, with full consciousness of what I was doing, to fuse political purpose and artistic purpose into one whole"; "Politics and the English Language" anatomises the linguistic operation the novella dramatises — slovenly abstract prose as the instrument of totalitarianism. Best fit: Q5 Squealer (the propaganda apparatus the essay diagnoses); Q7 Propaganda and Language (the central operation the essay names); Q10 Seven Commandments (the rewriting the essay analyses); Q4 Old Major (the diagnosis the essay defends).

2. Christopher Hitchens, Why Orwell Matters (2002). Hitchens reads Orwell as the writer who "got the three big questions of the twentieth century — empire, fascism and Stalinism — broadly right", and argues that Animal Farm is the most economical anti-totalitarian text in the English language because it refuses both Western Cold-War triumphalism and the consoling narrative that Stalinism was a deviation rather than the structural consequence of unaccountable revolutionary power. Best fit: Q1 Napoleon (Stalinism as structural rather than personal); Q2 Snowball (the suppression of the visionary intelligentsia); Q6 Power and Corruption (the structural diagnosis); Q9 Fear and Violence (the institutional permanence of the regime's foundation); Q8 Class and Inequality (the reproduction of hierarchy under new vocabulary).

A third option, useful as counter-voice for top-band students: Raymond Williams, Orwell (1971) — the less sympathetic Marxist reading that argues Orwell's anti-Stalinism is too easily co-opted by Cold-War politics; valuable for an AO3 counter-claim in the conclusion.

Final reminder

• One context factor per BP, threaded into the closing sentence so it lands the BP back at the controlling concept.

• One critical voice per essay, named with year, slotted into one BP or the conclusion.

• The controlling concept's three claims (every revolution that ignores the corruptibility of those who lead it ends as the tyranny it overthrew; power without accountability is the disease that recurs across regimes; language is the first thing the new tyranny corrupts) should land at least once across the introduction, BP topic sentences, or conclusion.

• Every Tech + evidence line uses ONE quote with multiple rhetorical techniques visible inside it. Grammar terms (passive construction, conditional, pluperfect) belong in close analysis only.

• Every effect verb varies and is specific to what the reader is doing in that line — hears / sees / feels / recognises / registers / infers / perceives — never receives.

• Every BP topic sentence inherits a connector from the preceding BP ("Where Chapters 1–4 had installed X, Chapters 5–7 reveal Y, Chapters 8–10 confirm Z") so the three BPs together trace the controlling argument's arc.

• Every conclusion's restated thesis retraces the chain using the same key nouns the BPs delivered.

• Sophicly's hamartia framing is "error in action", not "fatal flaw" — Old Major's hamartia is the failure to specify accountability mechanisms before the revolution acquired power.

• The diagnosis is honourable; the betrayal is historical, not inherent — Orwell is a "democratic socialist" (his own self-description in "Why I Write", 1946), and the novella is therefore not a critique of equality but a warning that equality must specify, in advance, the institutional design by which the leadership it produces will be checked.
